topic: [v15/zh/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] 请生成一份自由文本研究提案，研究免疫检查点抑制剂治疗非小细胞肺癌患者发生免疫相关不良事件的预测因素。需涵盖研究设计、样本纳入标准、统计分析计划和伦理考虑。
full_prompt: 请生成一份自由文本研究提案，研究免疫检查点抑制剂治疗非小细胞肺癌患者发生免疫相关不良事件的预测因素。需涵盖研究设计、样本纳入标准、统计分析计划和伦理考虑。
completion:
为形成研究提案，先检索近期关于非小细胞肺癌、免疫检查点抑制剂及免疫相关不良事件预测因素的临床研究证据。  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"non-small cell lung cancer immune checkpoint inhibitors immune-related adverse events predictors retrospective cohort PD-1 PD-L1 CTLA-4"}
```
[result]: 近年研究提示，NSCLC患者接受PD-1/PD-L1抑制剂后发生免疫相关不良事件（irAEs）可能与基线自身免疫病史、ECOG评分、年龄、性别、吸烟史、肿瘤负荷、PD-L1表达、外周血中性粒细胞/淋巴细胞比值（NLR）、嗜酸细胞计数、乳酸脱氢酶、白蛋白、联合治疗方案及既往放化疗有关。多数证据来自回顾性队列，预测模型仍需外部验证。

为细化统计方案，进一步阅读一项预测模型相关研究摘要。  
```action
{"tool":"read","source":"Recent cohort studies on predictors of immune-related adverse events in NSCLC patients treated with immune checkpoint inhibitors"}
```
[result]: 相关研究常采用回顾性或前瞻性队列设计，以CTCAE标准判定irAEs，主要终点包括任意级别irAEs和≥3级irAEs。统计方法包括单因素分析、多因素Logistic回归、Cox模型、LASSO变量筛选、ROC曲线、校准曲线和决策曲线分析。研究强调需处理混杂因素、缺失数据和时间依赖性偏倚。

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"本研究拟开展一项多中心、观察性队列研究，旨在识别接受免疫检查点抑制剂治疗的非小细胞肺癌患者发生免疫相关不良事件的预测因素，并建立临床可用的风险分层模型。研究对象为经病理或细胞学确诊的非小细胞肺癌患者，包括腺癌、鳞癌及其他少见亚型，接受PD-1、PD-L1或CTLA-4抑制剂单药或联合化疗、抗血管生成治疗的患者。主要研究终点为治疗开始后发生任意级别免疫相关不良事件，次要终点包括3级及以上免疫相关不良事件、特定器官毒性如免疫性肺炎、肝炎、肠炎、甲状腺炎，以及irAEs发生时间和因不良事件导致的停药率。\n\n样本纳入标准包括：年龄≥18岁；确诊为非小细胞肺癌；至少接受一次免疫检查点抑制剂治疗；治疗前具备完整的临床资料、实验室检查和影像学评估；随访时间不少于12周，除非在此期间发生死亡或严重不良事件。排除标准包括：治疗前已存在难以区分的活动性自身免疫性疾病急性发作；长期接受系统性免疫抑制治疗且无法停用者；合并其他活动性恶性肿瘤；关键基线资料缺失严重；无法进行不良事件归因评估者。拟收集变量包括人口学特征、吸烟史、ECOG评分、TNM分期、组织学类型、PD-L1表达、驱动基因状态、既往手术/放疗/化疗史、免疫治疗方案、合并用药，以及基线血常规、生化、甲状腺功能、炎症指标和自身抗体等。\n\n统计分析方面，首先对患者基线特征和irAEs发生情况进行描述性分析，连续变量根据分布采用均数±标准差或中位数及四分位数表示，分类变量采用频数和百分比表示。单因素分析将比较发生与未发生irAEs患者之间的差异，随后将具有临床意义或单因素分析P值较低的变量纳入多因素Logistic回归模型，以识别任意级别及重度irAEs的独立预测因素。对于irAEs发生时间，可采用Kaplan-Meier方法和Cox比例风险模型进行分析。若样本量允许，将使用LASSO回归进行变量筛选并建立预测评分或列线图，通过ROC曲线、校准曲线和决策曲线评估模型区分度、校准度和临床获益。缺失数据将根据缺失机制采用多重插补，并进行敏感性分析；同时考虑按治疗方案、PD-L1表达水平、病理类型和治疗线数进行亚组分析。\n\n伦理方面，本研究将遵循《赫尔辛基宣言》和当地临床研究伦理规范，研究方案需经各参与中心伦理委员会批准。若为回顾性研究，可申请知情同意豁免，但需确保患者隐私和数据匿名化；若包含前瞻性随访或额外采血检测，则需获得书面知情同意。所有数据将采用编码方式存储，仅限授权研究人员访问。研究不应干预患者常规诊疗决策，对发现的严重免疫相关不良事件应及时反馈临床团队。预期本研究将有助于早期识别irAEs高风险人群，优化随访频率和监测项目，提高免疫治疗安全性。"}
```

topic: [v15/zh/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] 针对重症监护病房脓毒症患者，构思一项研究以评估动态乳酸清除率联合炎症标志物对短期死亡风险的预测价值。请提出研究假设、数据收集方案和临床转化意义。
full_prompt: 针对重症监护病房脓毒症患者，构思一项研究以评估动态乳酸清除率联合炎症标志物对短期死亡风险的预测价值。请提出研究假设、数据收集方案和临床转化意义。
completion:
我将先快速检索既有证据，确认乳酸清除率、炎症标志物与脓毒症短期死亡风险之间的研究基础，再据此提出一个可实施的临床研究方案。

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"sepsis ICU lactate clearance inflammatory biomarkers short-term mortality prediction procalcitonin CRP IL-6"}
```

[result]: 模拟检索结果显示：动态乳酸清除率与脓毒症患者28天死亡率显著相关，早期乳酸清除不足通常提示组织灌注改善不佳；降钙素原、CRP、IL-6、中性粒细胞/淋巴细胞比值等炎症指标也与疾病严重程度及死亡风险相关。既往研究多单独评估乳酸或单个炎症指标，联合“动态代谢指标+炎症反应指标”构建短期死亡预测模型的研究仍有进一步优化空间。

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"本研究假设：在重症监护病房脓毒症患者中，入ICU后早期动态乳酸清除率联合炎症标志物水平，可较单独使用乳酸、SOFA评分或单一炎症指标更准确预测短期死亡风险，尤其是7天、14天及28天死亡。核心科学问题是：乳酸清除所反映的组织灌注恢复能力，与炎症标志物所反映的全身炎症负荷是否具有互补的预后信息。\n\n研究设计建议采用多中心前瞻性观察性队列研究。纳入符合Sepsis-3诊断标准、入住ICU后24小时内确诊的成年脓毒症或脓毒性休克患者；排除终末期恶性肿瘤、严重肝衰竭导致乳酸代谢显著异常、入ICU后24小时内放弃治疗者。收集基线人口学资料、感染部位、病原学结果、合并症、血流动力学指标、血管活性药物使用、机械通气、肾替代治疗、抗感染治疗时间窗，以及APACHE II和SOFA评分。\n\n关键暴露变量为动态乳酸清除率和炎症标志物。建议在入ICU时、6小时、12小时、24小时及48小时检测动脉乳酸，并计算6小时、12小时、24小时乳酸清除率；同步检测降钙素原、CRP、IL-6、白细胞计数、中性粒细胞/淋巴细胞比值等指标。主要结局为28天全因死亡，次要结局包括7天死亡、14天死亡、ICU死亡、ICU住院时间、休克持续时间及器官功能恶化。统计分析可采用多变量Logistic回归或Cox模型评估独立预测价值，并构建联合预测模型；通过ROC曲线、AUC、校准曲线、决策曲线分析、净重分类改善指数等方法评价模型性能，并进行内部验证或外部验证。\n\n临床转化意义在于形成一种可床旁实施的早期风险分层工具。若动态乳酸清除率联合炎症标志物能显著提高短期死亡预测能力，临床上可在入ICU后24小时内识别高危患者，推动更积极的感染源控制、抗感染治疗优化、血流动力学复苏和器官支持策略。同时，该模型还可用于ICU脓毒症患者的动态监测、预后沟通和临床试验分层入组，为精准化脓毒症管理提供依据。"}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/neuroscience] Write a free-text research proposal investigating how sleep-dependent synaptic plasticity supports memory consolidation in the hippocampus and neocortex.
full_prompt: Write a free-text research proposal investigating how sleep-dependent synaptic plasticity supports memory consolidation in the hippocampus and neocortex.
completion:
I’ll first gather a compact view of current findings linking sleep oscillations, synaptic plasticity, hippocampal replay, and neocortical consolidation, then use that to frame a proposal.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"sleep-dependent synaptic plasticity hippocampus neocortex memory consolidation sharp-wave ripples spindles slow oscillations review"}
```

[result]: Reviews and recent studies emphasize that hippocampal sharp-wave ripples during non-REM sleep coordinate with neocortical slow oscillations and thalamocortical spindles to support systems consolidation. Evidence also suggests sleep is not globally synaptic downscaling only; instead, it may combine selective synaptic strengthening for recently encoded memory traces with broader homeostatic renormalization. Open questions remain about how synapse-specific plasticity in hippocampal and neocortical circuits is coordinated across sleep stages and how this coordination predicts later memory performance.

To sharpen the proposal, I’ll inspect one representative source on hippocampal–neocortical communication during sleep.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"Representative review on hippocampal replay, sharp-wave ripples, slow oscillations, spindles, and systems memory consolidation during sleep"}
```

[result]: The source argues that non-REM sleep provides a temporal framework in which cortical slow oscillation up-states group thalamocortical spindles and hippocampal sharp-wave ripples, enabling reactivation of recently encoded memories. This coordinated replay may drive plasticity in distributed neocortical ensembles while gradually reducing hippocampal dependence. The review highlights the need for causal, cell-type-specific experiments measuring synaptic change across hippocampus and cortex before and after sleep.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will investigate how sleep-dependent synaptic plasticity supports memory consolidation across the hippocampus and neocortex. The central hypothesis is that non-REM sleep promotes memory consolidation through a coordinated two-part process: selective strengthening of synapses within task-relevant hippocampal–neocortical ensembles, coupled with broader synaptic renormalization that preserves network stability. The proposal focuses on how hippocampal sharp-wave ripples, neocortical slow oscillations, and sleep spindles interact to determine which synapses are stabilized, weakened, or transferred into longer-term cortical representations.\n\nThe study will use a rodent spatial memory paradigm combined with longitudinal in vivo calcium imaging, high-density electrophysiology, and post-sleep synaptic measurements. Animals will learn a novel spatial task, then undergo either normal sleep, selective disruption of hippocampal ripples, disruption of spindle–ripple coupling, or sleep deprivation. Neural ensembles active during learning will be tagged and followed across subsequent sleep and recall. The key outcome measures will be reactivation strength during sleep, changes in functional connectivity between hippocampal and neocortical ensembles, dendritic spine stability, and next-day memory performance.\n\nA second aim will test causality by manipulating sleep oscillations in a closed-loop manner. When cortical slow oscillation up-states are detected, optogenetic or auditory stimulation will either enhance or desynchronize the timing of spindles and hippocampal ripples. If precise temporal coupling is necessary for consolidation, enhancing coupling should increase replay fidelity, stabilize learning-associated spines, and improve recall, whereas disrupting coupling should impair cortical integration of the memory trace while leaving initial encoding intact.\n\nThis work will clarify whether sleep-dependent consolidation is best understood as simple synaptic downscaling, selective synaptic potentiation, or a coordinated balance between the two. By linking circuit-level sleep rhythms to synapse-specific plasticity and behavioral memory outcomes, the project will provide a mechanistic account of how hippocampal memories are reorganized into more durable neocortical representations. The findings could also inform interventions for memory impairment in aging, neurodegenerative disease, and sleep disorders."}
```

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"本研究拟设计为前瞻性、多中心、平行分组、随机对照临床试验，评价人工智能辅助结肠镜检查是否可提高成人筛查或诊断性结肠镜检查中的腺瘤检出率。纳入年龄40—75岁、拟接受择期结肠镜检查且肠道准备合格的受试者，排除炎症性肠病、遗传性息肉综合征、既往结直肠癌或结肠切除史、急诊内镜及凝血功能明显异常者。受试者按1:1随机分配至AI辅助结肠镜组或常规结肠镜组，采用中心化随机系统，并按研究中心、内镜医师及检查适应证进行分层，使用变长区组随机以减少分配预测。\n\nAI辅助组在常规高清结肠镜基础上开启实时病灶识别系统，系统对疑似息肉或腺瘤区域进行视觉提示；对照组使用同型号结肠镜但不启用AI提示。所有内镜操作者在研究开始前接受统一培训，包括研究方案、退镜观察规范、Boston肠道准备评分、病灶记录与取样/切除标准、AI系统界面和报警处理流程。参与医师需具备独立完成结肠镜检查资质，并提供既往操作量及基线ADR；正式入组前完成若干例试运行，以熟悉系统并降低学习曲线影响。病理医师对分组信息保持盲法。\n\n主要终点为腺瘤检出率，即每组至少检出1枚经病理证实腺瘤的受试者比例。次要终点包括息肉检出率、每例受试者腺瘤数、进展期腺瘤检出率、右半结肠腺瘤检出率、微小/小腺瘤检出率、盲肠插管率、退镜时间、总检查时间、活检或息肉切除次数、假阳性提示率、操作者间差异以及受试者满意度。可预设亚组分析，如不同肠道准备质量、检查适应证、内镜医师基线ADR水平和年龄分层。\n\n安全性评价包括检查相关穿孔、即时或延迟性出血、息肉切除后综合征、感染、误吸、麻醉或镇静相关不良事件、因AI提示导致的不必要活检/切除增加以及检查时间延长带来的风险。所有不良事件按严重程度、与操作及AI系统相关性进行判定，并设立独立数据安全监察委员会定期审查。研究假设AI辅助可在不显著增加严重不良事件的前提下提高ADR；样本量根据对照组预期ADR和最小临床有意义提升幅度计算，并按意向性分析为主、符合方案分析为辅。"}
```

[result]: 模拟阅读结果：建议妊娠期高血压疾病患者产后6–12周评估血压、血清肌酐/eGFR和尿白蛋白肌酐比；长期随访可每年或每2年重复检测。研究结局宜包括新发慢性肾病、eGFR持续下降、白蛋白尿/蛋白尿和终末期肾病。偏倚控制需重点处理基线肾功能缺失、检测频率差异、失访和妊娠前心肾代谢共病混杂。

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"本研究拟开展一项多中心回顾性-前瞻性结合的队列研究，分析妊娠期高血压疾病患者产后长期肾功能下降的风险因素。研究人群为在参与医院分娩、诊断为妊娠期高血压、子痫前期、重度子痫前期、子痫或慢性高血压合并子痫前期的孕产妇；纳入对象需有孕前或妊娠早期基础肾功能资料，或至少有产后6–12周肾功能检测。排除妊娠前已确诊终末期肾病、肾移植、系统性红斑狼疮活动期肾炎等明确严重肾脏疾病患者，但可将既往轻中度慢性肾病作为重要基线协变量或分层因素。\n\n主要暴露变量包括妊娠期高血压疾病类型及严重程度，如早发型子痫前期、重度高血压、蛋白尿程度、血小板减少、肝酶升高、血肌酐升高、肺水肿或神经系统症状等；同时收集孕周、分娩方式、早产、胎儿生长受限、胎盘早剥等妊娠结局。候选风险因素还应包括年龄、种族/地区、孕前BMI、吸烟、社会经济水平、既往高血压、糖尿病、慢性肾病、妊娠期糖尿病、产后血压控制情况、降压药使用、哺乳、再次妊娠及生活方式因素。\n\n结局定义建议采用复合与单项结局并行。主要结局为产后长期肾功能下降，可定义为产后一年以后出现eGFR较基线下降≥30%并持续至少3个月，或新发慢性肾病，即eGFR<60 ml/min/1.73m²持续3个月以上。次要结局包括新发白蛋白尿或蛋白尿，尿白蛋白肌酐比≥30 mg/g；eGFR年下降速率；新发高血压或产后持续高血压；以及终末期肾病、透析、肾移植等硬结局。\n\n随访策略为产后6–12周建立产后基线，之后在6个月、1年及其后每年随访，至少随访5–10年。每次随访测量标准化血压、血清肌酐、eGFR、尿白蛋白肌酐比、体重和用药情况，并通过电子病历、区域检验数据库和医保/死亡登记系统补充结局信息。对无法到院者可采用社区采血、尿样邮寄或与基层医疗机构协作，以减少失访。\n\n偏倚控制方面，应在设计阶段限制入排标准并统一妊娠期高血压疾病和肾脏结局定义；在分析阶段使用多变量Cox模型或混合效应模型校正年龄、BMI、孕前血压、糖尿病、基线eGFR和蛋白尿等混杂因素。对于检测频率差异导致的监测偏倚，可采用逆概率加权或联合建模；对失访采用多重插补和逆概率删失加权；对既往肾病和产后持续高血压进行分层及中介分析。此外，可设置阴性对照结局或与无妊娠期高血压的分娩人群匹配比较，以增强因果推断的稳健性。"}
```

topic: [v15/zh/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] 请撰写一份自由文本研究提案，比较远程心脏康复与传统门诊心脏康复在急性心肌梗死后患者中的疗效。关注依从性、运动耐量、再住院率和生活质量结局。
full_prompt: 请撰写一份自由文本研究提案，比较远程心脏康复与传统门诊心脏康复在急性心肌梗死后患者中的疗效。关注依从性、运动耐量、再住院率和生活质量结局。
completion:
我将先检索急性心肌梗死后心脏康复中远程/家庭模式与传统门诊模式的比较证据，重点提取依从性、运动耐量、再住院和生活质量等结局。随后基于证据缺口提出一个可执行的临床研究方案。  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"acute myocardial infarction telerehabilitation vs center-based cardiac rehabilitation adherence exercise capacity rehospitalization quality of life randomized trial meta-analysis"}
```  
[result]: 检索到多项系统综述和随机研究：远程心脏康复通常在运动能力（如6分钟步行距离、峰值VO2）方面不劣于传统门诊康复；部分研究显示远程模式因减少交通和时间障碍而提高参与率或完成率；再住院率结果总体相近但证据异质性较大；生活质量多采用SF-36、EQ-5D或MacNew量表，远程模式在心理社会维度可能有优势。证据缺口包括：AMI后早期患者的直接头对头非劣效研究不足、数字依从性客观测量不足、对高龄和农村患者的代表性不足、随访时间偏短。  

为细化研究设计，我将读取一篇相关综述/试验摘要，以确认常用终点、随访窗口和干预构成。  
```action
{"tool":"read","id":"telerehab_post_MI_meta_review_2023"}
```  
[result]: 该综述纳入AMI及冠心病患者心脏康复研究，远程干预通常包括可穿戴设备或手机应用监测、个体化运动处方、护士/康复师定期反馈和危险因素管理教育。常见随访为12周、6个月和12个月；主要运动终点包括峰值摄氧量、6分钟步行试验和心肺运动试验；依从性定义差异较大，包括登录次数、完成训练次数、达到处方运动量比例和项目完成率。作者建议未来研究采用标准化依从性指标，并评估临床事件和卫生服务利用。  

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"本研究拟开展一项多中心、随机、开放标签、非劣效临床试验，比较远程心脏康复与传统门诊心脏康复在急性心肌梗死后患者中的疗效与可及性。研究对象为AMI经PCI或药物治疗后病情稳定、出院后2周内可参与康复的成年人，按1:1随机分配至远程康复组或门诊康复组，干预持续12周，并随访至12个月。研究假设为：远程心脏康复在改善运动耐量方面不劣于传统门诊康复，同时可提高依从性，并在再住院率和生活质量方面达到相似或更优效果。\n\n远程康复组将接受基于手机应用和可穿戴设备的个体化运动处方，包括有氧运动、抗阻训练、心率监测、症状上报、用药与危险因素管理教育，以及每周线上康复师反馈；出现胸痛、异常心率或运动不耐受时启动预设安全流程。传统门诊组将接受标准院内或门诊心脏康复，包括现场监督运动训练、健康教育和危险因素控制。两组均接受同等频次的临床评估和二级预防指导，以尽量减少非康复模式本身造成的混杂。\n\n主要结局设定为12周时运动耐量变化，优先采用心肺运动试验峰值摄氧量；如部分中心不具备条件，可采用标准化6分钟步行距离作为关键次要运动结局。关键次要结局包括康复依从性，即完成处方训练次数比例、达到目标运动强度时间比例和项目完成率；6个月及12个月心血管相关再住院率；以及生活质量评分变化，采用EQ-5D-5L和MacNew心脏病生活质量量表。其他探索性结局包括血压、LDL-C、体重指数、吸烟状态、焦虑抑郁评分、患者满意度及医疗资源使用成本。\n\n统计分析将采用意向治疗和符合方案双重分析。主要终点以非劣效框架检验远程康复相对于门诊康复的运动耐量差异，非劣效界值根据既往心脏康复研究中临床最小重要差异确定；依从性和生活质量采用混合效应模型分析重复测量数据，再住院率采用Cox模型或竞争风险模型评估。预设亚组包括年龄、性别、城乡居住地、基线运动能力、数字设备使用能力和左室射血分数水平，以识别最可能从远程模式获益的人群。\n\n本研究的意义在于为AMI后患者提供一种兼顾疗效、安全性和可及性的康复模式证据。如果远程心脏康复被证实在运动耐量方面不劣于传统门诊康复，并能提升依从性、维持较低再住院风险和改善生活质量，将支持其作为标准心脏康复体系的重要补充，尤其适用于交通不便、工作时间受限或门诊康复资源不足的患者群体。"}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/neuroscience] Propose an experiment using optogenetics or chemogenetics to determine how specific amygdala neuronal populations contribute to fear extinction.
full_prompt: Propose an experiment using optogenetics or chemogenetics to determine how specific amygdala neuronal populations contribute to fear extinction.
completion:
I’ll first ground the proposal in established extinction-circuit findings, then use that to design a targeted causal experiment.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"amygdala neuronal populations fear extinction optogenetics basolateral amygdala intercalated cells central amygdala"}
```

[result]: Studies identify basolateral amygdala (BLA) excitatory ensembles, central amygdala (CeA) output pathways, and intercalated cell masses (ITCs) as key regulators of fear expression and extinction. Extinction learning is associated with altered activity in BLA projection-defined populations and inhibitory control over central amygdala fear-output neurons.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"projection-specific BLA neurons fear extinction infralimbic cortex nucleus accumbens central amygdala optogenetics"}
```

[result]: Prior work suggests that distinct BLA neuronal subpopulations can promote fear expression or fear suppression depending on projection target and activity state. Infralimbic prefrontal inputs and ITC-mediated inhibition are implicated in suppressing CeA-driven freezing after extinction.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"I propose an experiment to test whether projection-defined basolateral amygdala neurons causally regulate fear extinction. The central hypothesis is that BLA neurons projecting to the central amygdala promote fear expression, whereas BLA neurons engaged by infralimbic prefrontal or extinction-related circuits promote fear suppression. Adult mice would receive retrograde Cre virus in either the central amygdala or nucleus accumbens, combined with Cre-dependent channelrhodopsin or inhibitory opsins in the BLA, allowing selective manipulation of BLA projection populations during extinction learning and extinction recall.\n\nMice would undergo auditory fear conditioning using tone-shock pairings, followed 24 hours later by extinction training consisting of repeated tone presentations without shock. During extinction, one group would receive optogenetic activation of BLA→CeA neurons during each tone, another would receive optogenetic inhibition of the same population, and matched control mice would receive light without opsin expression. Freezing behavior would be measured during conditioning, extinction acquisition, and extinction recall the next day. If BLA→CeA neurons promote fear expression, their activation should impair extinction learning or maintain freezing, whereas inhibition should accelerate reduction of freezing during tone presentations.\n\nA parallel experiment would manipulate a candidate extinction-promoting population, such as BLA neurons projecting to nucleus accumbens or neurons recruited by infralimbic prefrontal input. These neurons could be activated during extinction training or selectively during extinction recall. If this population contributes to fear suppression, activation should enhance extinction acquisition or recall, while chemogenetic inhibition using inhibitory DREADDs should impair extinction recall and increase freezing despite prior extinction training.\n\nTo link manipulation effects to circuit mechanisms, fiber photometry or calcium imaging could be used in a separate cohort to record activity from the same projection-defined populations across conditioning, extinction, and recall. The prediction is that fear-promoting BLA→CeA neurons will be most active during high-freezing trials, while extinction-promoting populations will increase activity as animals learn that the tone no longer predicts shock. Together, these experiments would establish whether distinct amygdala neuronal populations have opposing causal roles in maintaining fear versus supporting extinction, providing mechanistic insight relevant to anxiety and trauma-related disorders."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/neuroscience] Design a study to test whether gut microbiome composition influences anxiety-related neural circuits through vagal signaling.
full_prompt: Design a study to test whether gut microbiome composition influences anxiety-related neural circuits through vagal signaling.
completion:
To ground the study in prior neuroscience evidence, I’ll first identify work linking microbiome manipulations, anxiety behavior, vagal signaling, and brain circuits.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"gut microbiome anxiety neural circuits vagus nerve vagotomy mice GABA amygdala prefrontal cortex"}
```

[result]: Simulated search result: Key prior work includes Bravo et al. 2011 showing that *Lactobacillus rhamnosus* altered anxiety- and depression-related behavior and region-specific GABA receptor expression in mice, effects abolished by subdiaphragmatic vagotomy. Related studies implicate germ-free mice, antibiotic depletion, fecal microbiota transplant, and vagal afferents in microbiota-gut-brain signaling. Relevant circuits include basolateral amygdala, medial prefrontal cortex, hippocampus, nucleus tractus solitarius, and hypothalamic stress pathways.

I’ll inspect the most directly relevant study to extract design principles and limitations for a stronger causal experiment.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"Bravo 2011 Lactobacillus rhamnosus vagus nerve emotional behavior GABA receptor expression"}
```

[result]: Simulated read result: Bravo et al. administered *L. rhamnosus* JB-1 to mice and found reduced anxiety-like and depression-like behavior, altered GABA receptor mRNA expression across cortical, hippocampal, and amygdalar regions, and reduced stress-induced corticosterone. Subdiaphragmatic vagotomy prevented these effects, supporting vagal mediation. Limitations: single probiotic strain, limited circuit-level physiology, no cell-type-specific vagal manipulation, and microbiome composition was not treated as a naturally varying causal predictor.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"I propose a mouse study testing whether naturally distinct gut microbiome compositions causally alter anxiety-related neural circuits through vagal signaling. Adult germ-free mice would be colonized with fecal microbiota from either high-anxiety or low-anxiety donor mice, identified through elevated plus maze, open field, and light-dark box screening. A parallel group would receive defined microbial consortia enriched for taxa or metabolites associated with each donor phenotype. This design separates microbiome composition from host genetics and allows direct testing of whether microbiota transfer is sufficient to shift anxiety-like behavior.\n\nTo test vagal dependence, colonized mice would be assigned to one of three vagal-signaling conditions: sham surgery, subdiaphragmatic vagotomy, or chemogenetic silencing of vagal afferent neurons using Phox2b- or Nav1.8-linked inhibitory DREADDs. This would allow comparison between broad surgical interruption and more selective, reversible suppression of vagal sensory signaling. The main prediction is that high-anxiety microbiota will increase anxiety-like behavior and alter activity in anxiety circuits under sham conditions, but these effects will be reduced or abolished when vagal afferent signaling is disrupted.\n\nNeural circuit outcomes would be measured at behavioral baseline and after mild anxiogenic challenge. Fiber photometry or miniscope calcium imaging would record activity in basolateral amygdala projections to medial prefrontal cortex and ventral hippocampus, while c-Fos mapping and immediate early gene profiling would assess broader circuit recruitment, including nucleus tractus solitarius, parabrachial nucleus, amygdala, hippocampus, and prefrontal cortex. Electrophysiology in acute slices could test whether microbiota condition changes excitatory-inhibitory balance in basolateral amygdala and infralimbic/prelimbic prefrontal cortex. These neural measures would be paired with 16S rRNA sequencing, shotgun metagenomics, and targeted metabolomics for short-chain fatty acids, tryptophan metabolites, bile acids, and GABA-related metabolites.\n\nThe central analysis would test whether microbiome composition predicts anxiety behavior and circuit activity, and whether vagal disruption statistically mediates or blocks these relationships. If microbiota-induced anxiety phenotypes and amygdala-prefrontal circuit changes disappear after vagotomy or vagal-afferent silencing, this would support a causal vagal pathway. If behavioral effects persist despite vagal disruption, the study would suggest alternative routes such as immune, endocrine, or microbial metabolite signaling across the blood-brain barrier. This design extends prior probiotic work by using causal microbiota transfer, selective vagal manipulation, and direct circuit-level neural readouts."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/neuroscience] Develop a research plan to investigate whether neuroinflammation accelerates tau pathology in Alzheimer’s disease models.
full_prompt: Develop a research plan to investigate whether neuroinflammation accelerates tau pathology in Alzheimer’s disease models.
completion:
To ground the plan in current evidence, I’ll first search for recent work linking inflammatory activation, microglia, cytokines, and tau propagation/pathology in Alzheimer’s disease models.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"neuroinflammation accelerates tau pathology Alzheimer disease mouse models microglia cytokines tau propagation"}
```

[result]: Recent studies suggest that microglial activation, inflammasome signaling, complement pathways, and cytokines such as IL-1β and TNF-α can exacerbate tau phosphorylation, aggregation, and spread in transgenic tau and amyloid/tau mouse models. Evidence also implicates TREM2-dependent microglial states and peripheral inflammatory challenges as modifiers of tau pathology, though causal mechanisms and timing remain incompletely resolved.

I’ll read a representative source focused on causal inflammatory mechanisms and tau pathology progression.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"microglial inflammasome IL-1 beta tau pathology Alzheimer mouse model"}
```

[result]: A representative body of work indicates that activation of innate immune pathways, including NLRP3 inflammasome signaling and IL-1β release, can enhance tau kinase activity, tau phosphorylation, synaptic dysfunction, and neurodegeneration. Pharmacologic or genetic suppression of inflammatory signaling has been reported to reduce tau pathology in some models, but effects vary depending on disease stage, tau model, sex, and whether inflammation is acute or chronic.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will test the hypothesis that neuroinflammation accelerates tau pathology in Alzheimer’s disease models by increasing tau phosphorylation, aggregation, and trans-synaptic spread through microglial cytokine and inflammasome pathways. We will use complementary in vivo and ex vivo systems: a tauopathy mouse model such as PS19 or P301S, an amyloid-plus-tau model if available, and primary neuron–microglia co-cultures seeded with pathological tau. The central prediction is that experimentally induced inflammation will worsen tau burden and neurodegeneration, whereas anti-inflammatory or pathway-specific interventions will attenuate these outcomes.\n\nAim 1 will determine whether inflammatory activation accelerates tau pathology over time. Tau-transgenic mice will receive either a chronic low-dose inflammatory challenge, such as systemic LPS or intracerebral inflammatory stimulation, or vehicle control at pre-symptomatic and early symptomatic stages. Brains will be collected longitudinally and assessed for phospho-tau, insoluble tau, tau seeding activity, microglial activation, astrocytosis, cytokine profiles, synaptic markers, and neuronal loss. Behavioral assays of memory and motor function will test whether inflammation-linked tau acceleration corresponds to functional decline.\n\nAim 2 will identify inflammatory mechanisms that mediate tau acceleration. We will focus on candidate pathways including NLRP3 inflammasome signaling, IL-1β, TNF-α, complement activation, and disease-associated microglial states. Mice or cultures will be treated with pathway-specific inhibitors or crossed with relevant knockout lines where feasible, then challenged with inflammatory stimuli or tau seeds. Single-cell or spatial transcriptomic profiling of microglia and astrocytes will be used to determine whether particular inflammatory cell states correlate with tau spread and neuronal vulnerability.\n\nAim 3 will test whether neuroinflammation enhances tau propagation rather than only local tau phosphorylation. Misfolded tau seeds will be injected into a defined brain region, such as hippocampus or entorhinal cortex, in animals with high versus low inflammatory tone. Subsequent tau spread to connected regions will be quantified using phospho-tau immunostaining, biochemical fractionation, and tau seeding assays. Parallel neuron–microglia co-cultures will allow mechanistic testing of whether inflammatory microglia increase neuronal tau uptake, tau release, or extracellular vesicle-mediated propagation.\n\nThe expected outcome is a causal map linking defined inflammatory pathways to specific stages of tau pathology: phosphorylation, aggregation, spread, and neurodegeneration. This research will clarify whether neuroinflammation is merely a response to tau pathology or an active driver of disease progression. If inflammatory blockade reduces tau acceleration, the findings would support therapeutic strategies targeting microglial or cytokine pathways during early Alzheimer’s disease, before widespread tau-mediated neurodegeneration becomes irreversible."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/neuroscience] Write a free-text research proposal on using human brain organoids to model early neurodevelopmental changes associated with autism spectrum disorder.
full_prompt: Write a free-text research proposal on using human brain organoids to model early neurodevelopmental changes associated with autism spectrum disorder.
completion:
Rationale: I will first search for recent neuroscience literature on ASD-associated brain organoid models to ground the proposal in current methods and knowledge gaps.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"human brain organoids autism spectrum disorder early neurodevelopment cortical organoids iPSC recent review"}
```

[result]: Recent literature highlights that iPSC-derived cortical and forebrain organoids from individuals with ASD or ASD-associated mutations can model early neurodevelopmental phenotypes including altered neural progenitor proliferation, excitatory/inhibitory imbalance, abnormal neurogenesis timing, dysregulated mTOR/WNT/CHD8 pathways, and changes in synaptogenesis. Key limitations include organoid variability, immature cell states, lack of vascularization and sensory input, and the need for isogenic controls, single-cell multiomics, and functional validation.

Rationale: I will read one representative source to identify a sharper hypothesis and feasible experimental design.

```action
{"tool":"read","citation":"Recent review on iPSC-derived brain organoids for autism spectrum disorder modeling"}
```

[result]: The review emphasizes that ASD likely emerges from disruptions in early cortical patterning, progenitor dynamics, neuronal subtype specification, and circuit assembly. It recommends combining patient-derived and CRISPR-engineered isogenic organoids, longitudinal single-cell RNA-seq/ATAC-seq, spatial transcriptomics, calcium imaging or multielectrode arrays, and rescue experiments targeting candidate pathways.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project proposes to use human induced pluripotent stem cell-derived brain organoids to model early neurodevelopmental alterations associated with autism spectrum disorder (ASD). The central hypothesis is that ASD risk variants converge on a limited set of early developmental processes—neural progenitor proliferation, cortical neuronal subtype specification, excitatory/inhibitory balance, and early network maturation—that can be detected in three-dimensional human forebrain organoids before overt synaptic phenotypes emerge. By focusing on early developmental time points, the study aims to identify cellular and molecular changes that precede later circuit-level dysfunction.\n\nWe will generate dorsal forebrain organoids from iPSC lines derived from individuals with ASD carrying well-characterized genetic risk factors, alongside unaffected controls and CRISPR-corrected or CRISPR-engineered isogenic pairs. Organoids will be sampled longitudinally across stages corresponding to early cortical development, with assays including immunohistochemistry for progenitor and neuronal markers, EdU-based proliferation analysis, single-cell RNA sequencing, single-cell ATAC sequencing, and spatial transcriptomics. These approaches will allow us to determine whether ASD-associated organoids show altered radial glia expansion, premature or delayed neurogenesis, changes in deep- and upper-layer cortical neuron production, or disrupted trajectories of interneuron-related and synaptogenic programs.\n\nTo connect molecular phenotypes with emerging function, we will complement the developmental profiling with calcium imaging and multielectrode array recordings as organoids mature. Particular attention will be paid to signatures of excitatory/inhibitory imbalance, altered spontaneous activity, and abnormal network synchrony. Computational integration of transcriptomic, epigenomic, spatial, and physiological data will be used to identify convergent ASD-associated pathways across genetically distinct lines, while distinguishing shared mechanisms from variant-specific effects.\n\nFinally, candidate pathways identified from the organoid analyses—such as mTOR, WNT, chromatin remodeling, or activity-dependent synaptic signaling—will be tested through targeted rescue experiments using pharmacological modulation or genetic correction. Successful rescue of abnormal progenitor behavior, neuronal specification, or early network activity would provide evidence that these pathways are causally involved in ASD-relevant neurodevelopmental changes. Overall, this study would establish a human-specific experimental framework for linking ASD genetic risk to early cortical developmental mechanisms, with the long-term goal of identifying biomarkers and therapeutic windows before stable circuit abnormalities are established."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/neuroscience] Write a free-text research proposal exploring how noninvasive brain stimulation could enhance motor recovery after stroke.
full_prompt: Write a free-text research proposal exploring how noninvasive brain stimulation could enhance motor recovery after stroke.
completion:
Rationale: I’ll first scan for recent evidence on noninvasive brain stimulation for post-stroke motor recovery, then use that to frame a focused neuroscience research proposal.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"noninvasive brain stimulation motor recovery after stroke tDCS rTMS rehabilitation randomized trial mechanisms"}
```

[result]: Recent literature highlights transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) and repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) as promising adjuncts to motor rehabilitation after stroke. Key themes include rebalancing interhemispheric inhibition, enhancing corticospinal excitability, pairing stimulation with task-specific training, and identifying biomarkers such as motor evoked potentials, lesion location, and functional connectivity to predict treatment response.

Rationale: I’ll read a representative review/trial summary to extract mechanistic and design considerations for the proposal.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"Review summary on tDCS and rTMS for upper-limb motor recovery after stroke"}
```

[result]: The source emphasizes that stimulation effects are heterogeneous across patients. Excitatory stimulation of ipsilesional motor cortex and inhibitory stimulation of contralesional motor cortex are common strategies, but optimal protocols may depend on stroke chronicity, lesion burden, residual corticospinal tract integrity, and baseline network connectivity. Combining stimulation with intensive, goal-directed motor practice appears more effective than stimulation alone.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will investigate how noninvasive brain stimulation can enhance upper-limb motor recovery after stroke by promoting adaptive plasticity in surviving motor networks. Stroke often disrupts corticospinal output and alters the balance of excitability between hemispheres, limiting recovery despite conventional rehabilitation. We propose that individually targeted stimulation, paired with task-specific motor training, will improve motor outcomes more reliably than a one-size-fits-all stimulation approach.\n\nThe study will enroll adults with subacute ischemic stroke and persistent unilateral upper-limb weakness. Participants will be randomized to receive either personalized noninvasive brain stimulation plus intensive motor therapy or sham stimulation plus the same therapy. Stimulation will be delivered using either excitatory anodal tDCS or facilitatory rTMS over ipsilesional motor cortex, or inhibitory stimulation over contralesional motor cortex, selected according to each participant’s baseline neurophysiology. Motor evoked potentials, corticospinal tract integrity from diffusion MRI, and resting-state functional connectivity will guide stimulation selection.\n\nThe central hypothesis is that stimulation will improve recovery by increasing ipsilesional corticospinal excitability, reducing maladaptive interhemispheric inhibition, and strengthening functional connectivity within motor networks. Outcomes will include changes in Fugl-Meyer upper-extremity scores, grip strength, kinematic measures of reaching, and neurophysiological markers measured before treatment, immediately after treatment, and at three-month follow-up. This design will allow us to test both clinical efficacy and mechanistic pathways of recovery.\n\nA major innovation of the project is its precision-medicine framework. Rather than assuming all stroke survivors benefit from the same stimulation montage, the study will match stimulation strategy to individual brain physiology. If successful, this work could identify biomarkers that predict who benefits from noninvasive brain stimulation and establish a practical protocol for integrating brain stimulation into neurorehabilitation. Ultimately, the findings could help transform post-stroke therapy from generalized exercise-based rehabilitation toward targeted, brain-guided recovery interventions."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/neuroscience] What neural mechanisms allow the brain to integrate visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive signals during spatial navigation?
full_prompt: What neural mechanisms allow the brain to integrate visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive signals during spatial navigation?
completion:
To ground the proposal, I’ll first search for recent work on multisensory integration in spatial navigation, focusing on hippocampal-entorhinal, retrosplenial, parietal, and vestibular pathways.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"neural mechanisms visual vestibular proprioceptive integration spatial navigation hippocampus entorhinal retrosplenial cortex"}
```

[result]: Recent literature highlights that spatial navigation depends on interactions among vestibular nuclei, thalamus, retrosplenial cortex, posterior parietal cortex, hippocampus, and medial entorhinal cortex. Grid cells, place cells, head-direction cells, and border cells combine self-motion/path-integration signals with visual landmarks. Retrosplenial cortex is repeatedly implicated in transforming egocentric sensory inputs into allocentric spatial maps. Vestibular disruption impairs hippocampal theta rhythms, place-cell stability, and path integration.

I’ll read a representative synthesis on how self-motion and landmark cues are combined across navigation circuits.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"review multisensory integration path integration landmarks vestibular visual proprioceptive hippocampal entorhinal navigation"}
```

[result]: The review argues that navigation relies on dynamic cue weighting: vestibular and proprioceptive signals support idiothetic self-motion estimates, while vision anchors these estimates to external landmarks. Entorhinal grid-cell firing can be updated by path integration but corrected by visual boundaries and landmarks. Retrosplenial and posterior parietal cortices may mediate coordinate transformations between body-centered movement signals and world-centered spatial representations. Neuromodulatory and oscillatory mechanisms, especially theta-band synchronization, may regulate communication between these systems.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will investigate how the brain integrates visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive signals to support spatial navigation. I propose that navigation depends on a distributed multisensory circuit in which vestibular and proprioceptive inputs provide continuous self-motion estimates, visual cues provide external error correction, and retrosplenial–entorhinal–hippocampal networks combine these signals into stable allocentric spatial maps. A central hypothesis is that cue integration is not fixed, but dynamically reweighted according to cue reliability, behavioral context, and accumulated path-integration error.\n\nThe first aim will test how visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive cues influence spatial coding in hippocampal and entorhinal neurons. Animals could navigate in a virtual or augmented-reality environment in which optic flow, treadmill/body-motion feedback, and vestibular stimulation are independently manipulated. Simultaneous recordings from place cells, grid cells, head-direction cells, and retrosplenial neurons would reveal whether different cell types preferentially track external landmarks, self-motion cues, or conflict between modalities.\n\nThe second aim will examine the circuit mechanisms that transform body-centered sensory signals into world-centered spatial representations. Retrosplenial cortex and posterior parietal cortex are strong candidates for this computation because they receive multimodal sensory and motor-related information and project to hippocampal–entorhinal navigation circuits. Targeted perturbation of these regions during cue-conflict tasks would test whether they are necessary for aligning egocentric movement signals with allocentric spatial maps.\n\nThe third aim will identify temporal coordination mechanisms supporting multisensory integration. I predict that theta-band synchronization between vestibular/parietal/retrosplenial regions and hippocampal–entorhinal circuits increases when animals must combine self-motion with visual landmarks, and that disrupting this coordination will destabilize spatial firing. Together, these experiments would clarify how the brain builds a coherent navigational estimate from partially redundant and sometimes conflicting visual, vestibular, and proprioceptive signals."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Propose a research plan for safe reinforcement learning in human-robot collaborative control, including methods for formal safety guarantees and evaluation in s
full_prompt: Propose a research plan for safe reinforcement learning in human-robot collaborative control, including methods for formal safety guarantees and evaluation in shared workspaces.
completion:
To ground the proposal in current safe-RL and human-robot collaboration methods, I’ll first search for recent work combining reinforcement learning, control barrier functions, and shared-workspace evaluation.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"safe reinforcement learning human robot collaboration control barrier functions shared workspace formal safety guarantees"}
```

[result]: Recent literature highlights several relevant directions: safe reinforcement learning with control barrier functions (CBFs) or Hamilton-Jacobi reachability as runtime safety filters; constrained Markov decision process methods for learning under collision-risk constraints; formal verification of learned controllers using reachability or temporal logic; and human-robot collaboration benchmarks involving speed-and-separation monitoring, handover, co-assembly, and shared manipulation tasks.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"control barrier functions safe reinforcement learning human robot interaction formal guarantees"}
```

[result]: Key themes include using CBF-based quadratic programs to minimally modify learned robot actions so that safety constraints remain forward invariant; modeling humans probabilistically to handle uncertainty in motion prediction; combining offline verification with online shielding; and evaluating safety through minimum separation distance, intervention frequency, task efficiency, comfort, and robustness to out-of-distribution human behavior.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"I propose a research program on safe reinforcement learning for human-robot collaborative control in which a robot learns efficient shared-workspace behavior while a formally verified safety layer guarantees collision avoidance and bounded interaction forces. The central hypothesis is that task-level adaptability can be learned with reinforcement learning, while hard safety constraints should be enforced through model-based runtime shields rather than left entirely to reward shaping. The target domains will include collaborative assembly, handover, and shared-object manipulation in workspaces where human motion is uncertain and partially observable.\n\nThe technical approach will combine constrained reinforcement learning with formal safety filters. A learned policy will optimize task performance, human comfort, and coordination efficiency, while a control barrier function or reachability-based shield will project each proposed action into a certified safe set. Safety constraints will include minimum human-robot separation, velocity-dependent protective zones, joint torque and force limits, and workspace exclusion regions. To address uncertainty in human behavior, the safety filter will use probabilistic human motion prediction with confidence-bounded reachable sets; the formal guarantee will be stated as forward invariance of a conservative safe set under bounded prediction error. For richer temporal requirements, such as “do not enter the handover zone until the human is ready,” the project will also encode specifications in signal temporal logic and verify learned policies against these constraints offline.\n\nThe learning architecture will use a two-level controller: a high-level RL policy for collaborative intent selection and motion objectives, and a low-level model-predictive or CBF-QP controller for dynamically feasible safe execution. Training will begin in simulation with domain randomization over human trajectories, reaction times, occlusions, and task layouts. The reward will include task completion, smoothness, legibility, and human-effort reduction, while constraints will be handled using a constrained Markov decision process formulation. Importantly, the final deployed policy will not rely on learned safety alone; instead, all executed commands must pass through the certified safety shield.\n\nEvaluation will proceed in three stages. First, simulation experiments will compare unconstrained RL, reward-penalized RL, constrained RL, and shielded RL under nominal and adversarial human motion. Second, formal analysis will report certified safe-set size, robustness margins, and cases where the shield must override the learner. Third, physical experiments will be conducted with a collaborative robot in a marked shared workspace using representative assembly and handover tasks. Metrics will include collision and near-miss rate, minimum separation distance, force-limit violations, task completion time, intervention frequency, number of shield activations, human perceived safety, workload, and trust. The expected contribution is a safe-RL framework that preserves the adaptability of learning-based control while providing explicit, auditable safety guarantees suitable for human-robot collaborative workcells."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Develop a study on whole-body control for humanoid robots that balances locomotion, manipulation, and push recovery using hierarchical optimization.
full_prompt: Develop a study on whole-body control for humanoid robots that balances locomotion, manipulation, and push recovery using hierarchical optimization.
completion:
I’ll first look for recent framing around hierarchical whole-body control for humanoids, especially where locomotion, manipulation, and disturbance recovery are optimized together.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"hierarchical whole-body control humanoid locomotion manipulation push recovery quadratic programming"}
```

[result]: Recent work emphasizes hierarchical quadratic programming, task-priority inverse dynamics, centroidal momentum regulation, contact wrench optimization, and model predictive control for humanoid whole-body behaviors. Common gaps include weak integration between manipulation goals and balance recovery, brittle priority switching during contacts or pushes, and limited validation on simultaneous walking, reaching, and disturbance rejection.

To ground the proposal, I’ll read a representative source on task-priority whole-body control and contact-consistent optimization.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"survey/recent paper on hierarchical inverse-dynamics whole-body control for torque-controlled humanoids"}
```

[result]: The source describes a stack-of-tasks formulation where high-priority constraints enforce rigid-body dynamics, contact consistency, torque/friction limits, and balance safety, while lower-priority objectives track locomotion, posture, and end-effector tasks. It highlights open challenges in dynamically adjusting priorities, handling unexpected pushes without sacrificing manipulation, and coordinating centroidal momentum with contact transitions.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This study will develop a hierarchical optimization framework for humanoid whole-body control that jointly balances locomotion, manipulation, and push recovery. The central hypothesis is that robustness improves when balance is not treated as a separate reflex layer, but as a high-priority, continuously active optimization objective coupled to locomotion and manipulation through centroidal momentum, contact wrench feasibility, and task-priority adaptation.\n\nThe proposed controller will use a hierarchical quadratic programming architecture. The highest priority level will enforce full-body rigid-body dynamics, unilateral contact constraints, friction cones, torque limits, and joint limits. The next level will regulate centroidal momentum, center-of-mass motion, and support-polygon or capture-region safety for push recovery. Lower levels will track locomotion objectives such as swing-foot trajectories and pelvis motion, while simultaneously tracking manipulation objectives such as hand pose, interaction force, or object stabilization. A key contribution will be an adaptive priority and weighting policy that temporarily reshapes the hierarchy under disturbances, allowing the robot to preserve manipulation goals when possible while sacrificing them gracefully when balance is threatened.\n\nThe experimental study will evaluate the controller in simulated and hardware humanoid scenarios involving walking while carrying an object, reaching during stepping, and recovering from lateral and sagittal pushes. Baselines will include a fixed-priority whole-body controller, a locomotion-only push recovery controller, and a manipulation-focused controller with balance constraints. Metrics will include fall rate, center-of-mass/capture-point deviation, task tracking error, contact wrench feasibility, recovery time, energy or torque cost, and manipulation success after disturbance.\n\nThe expected outcome is a unified control method that enables humanoid robots to handle competing whole-body objectives without brittle mode switching. By embedding push recovery, locomotion, and manipulation into a single hierarchical optimization stack, the study aims to show that humanoids can maintain task performance under realistic physical disturbances while respecting contact and actuation limits. This would contribute to robust humanoid deployment in human environments, where walking, object interaction, and unexpected contacts often occur simultaneously."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Write a free-text research proposal on adaptive model predictive control for legged robots traversing deformable terrain, emphasizing real-time constraint handl
full_prompt: Write a free-text research proposal on adaptive model predictive control for legged robots traversing deformable terrain, emphasizing real-time constraint handling and uncertainty estimation.
completion:
I’ll first gather a compact view of recent work linking legged locomotion MPC, terrain adaptation, and uncertainty-aware control so the proposal can target a clear research gap.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"adaptive model predictive control legged robots deformable terrain uncertainty estimation real-time constraints"}
```

[result]: Recent work highlights nonlinear and convex MPC for quadruped locomotion, terrain-aware gait planning, and learned residual dynamics for contact-rich control. However, deformable terrain remains challenging because contact parameters such as sinkage, shear strength, friction, and stiffness vary online and are only partially observable. Common gaps include limited real-time uncertainty propagation inside MPC, conservative constraint handling, and insufficient integration of proprioceptive terrain estimation with adaptive contact models.

I’ll read one representative source summary to sharpen the proposal around a specific technical contribution.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"summary of MPC for legged robots on deformable terrain with terrain parameter estimation"}
```

[result]: Representative approaches combine reduced-order centroidal dynamics with MPC, estimating terrain/contact parameters from force, motion, and slip observations. Some methods adapt friction or foothold costs, while others use learned terrain classifiers. Few jointly estimate uncertainty over deformable contact parameters and embed that uncertainty into real-time state/input/contact constraints. Promising directions include tube MPC, chance constraints, differentiable contact models, online Bayesian estimation, and fast QP/SOCP solvers for embedded control.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This research proposes an adaptive model predictive control framework for legged robots traversing deformable terrain such as sand, mud, snow, and granular soil. The central objective is to enable robust, agile locomotion when terrain properties are unknown, spatially varying, and changing under repeated foot contact. The project will develop a real-time MPC controller that couples reduced-order robot dynamics with an adaptive deformable-contact model, allowing the controller to update its assumptions about sinkage, slip, friction, and ground stiffness during locomotion rather than relying on fixed conservative parameters.\n\nThe first thrust will focus on online uncertainty estimation for terrain-contact dynamics. Using proprioceptive signals including joint torques, foot forces, base acceleration, foot penetration, and slip velocity, the system will infer latent terrain parameters through a lightweight Bayesian estimator or ensemble residual model. Instead of producing a single terrain estimate, the estimator will maintain uncertainty bounds over friction, compliance, and shear response. These uncertainty estimates will be passed directly to the MPC layer, allowing the controller to distinguish between well-characterized terrain and regions where more cautious behavior is required.\n\nThe second thrust will develop real-time constraint handling methods for adaptive locomotion MPC. The controller will encode state, input, contact-force, friction-cone, foothold, and stability constraints while accounting for uncertainty in terrain parameters. We will investigate chance-constrained and tube-based MPC formulations that preserve safety without becoming overly conservative. To meet onboard compute limits, the project will use reduced-order centroidal dynamics, structured optimization, warm-started solvers, and constraint tightening schemes that can run at locomotion control rates. A key research question is how to balance constraint satisfaction, agility, and computational tractability when terrain uncertainty changes at every step.\n\nThe third thrust will integrate terrain adaptation with gait and foothold selection. The MPC will not only regulate body motion and contact forces, but also modify step timing, foothold placement, and gait transitions in response to estimated deformability. For example, the robot may lengthen stance time on high-sinkage terrain, avoid regions with high slip uncertainty, or redistribute contact forces to reduce ground failure. This will be evaluated in simulation and on a quadruped robot over controlled deformable substrates with varying moisture, compaction, slope, and granular composition.\n\nExpected contributions include a unified adaptive MPC architecture for deformable terrain locomotion, a real-time uncertainty estimator for contact parameters, and constraint-handling algorithms that provide measurable safety and performance guarantees under terrain uncertainty. Success will be measured by reduced slip and sinkage, improved tracking, fewer constraint violations, and faster traversal compared with fixed-parameter MPC and heuristic terrain-adaptive baselines. The broader impact is a control methodology that enables legged robots to operate more reliably in disaster response, planetary exploration, agriculture, construction, and field inspection environments where rigid-ground assumptions routinely fail."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Investigate how diffusion-based policy priors can be combined with classical feedback control to improve manipulation robustness under perception noise and cont
full_prompt: Investigate how diffusion-based policy priors can be combined with classical feedback control to improve manipulation robustness under perception noise and contact uncertainty.
completion:
I’ll first look for recent work connecting diffusion policies, visuomotor manipulation, and feedback/contact-rich control to identify a concrete research gap.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"diffusion policy manipulation feedback control contact uncertainty perception noise robotics"}
```

[result]: Recent relevant themes include: diffusion policies for visuomotor manipulation that model multimodal action distributions; receding-horizon diffusion policy execution; combining learned policies with model predictive control; residual policy learning over impedance or operational-space controllers; contact-rich manipulation under state-estimation uncertainty; and robust force/position hybrid control. A recurring gap is that diffusion policies often produce open-loop action chunks or weakly feedback-corrected trajectories, while classical controllers provide stability and disturbance rejection but lack rich priors over multimodal manipulation strategies.

To refine the proposal, I’ll inspect a representative diffusion-policy paper and related hybrid-control ideas.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"Diffusion Policy Visuomotor Policy Learning via Action Diffusion receding horizon manipulation"}
```

[result]: Diffusion Policy represents visuomotor actions as conditional denoising samples, often producing short action horizons executed in receding-horizon fashion. It performs well on manipulation tasks with multimodal behavior and high-dimensional observations, but robustness can degrade under visual occlusion, pose noise, unexpected contact, or dynamics mismatch. Related classical-control approaches such as impedance control, hybrid force/position control, tube MPC, and disturbance observers provide feedback robustness but generally require task-specific models or nominal references.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will investigate a hybrid manipulation architecture in which a diffusion-based policy serves as a high-level, multimodal action prior while a classical feedback controller provides real-time stabilization against perception noise and contact uncertainty. The core hypothesis is that diffusion policies are most useful for proposing feasible, task-conditioned motion/contact strategies, whereas feedback control should be responsible for enforcing local stability, compliance, and constraint satisfaction during execution.\n\nThe proposed method will use a conditional diffusion model to generate short-horizon end-effector, force, or impedance-parameter trajectories from noisy visual and proprioceptive observations. Instead of executing these samples directly, the system will embed them as priors inside a feedback controller, such as operational-space impedance control, hybrid force/position control, or tube MPC. The controller will track the denoised nominal trajectory while adapting gains, contact forces, or allowable uncertainty tubes according to predicted diffusion uncertainty and online state-estimation confidence.\n\nA key research component will be uncertainty-aware fusion between the learned prior and the feedback layer. Diffusion sample diversity can be interpreted as a local distribution over plausible manipulation strategies; high sample agreement would permit aggressive tracking, while high disagreement or poor perception confidence would trigger more compliant behavior, slower motions, or contact-seeking exploration. Contact events will be handled by switching or continuously blending between position-dominant and force-dominant control modes, using residual errors and tactile/force signals to correct the diffusion prior online.\n\nExperiments will evaluate the approach on contact-rich manipulation tasks such as peg insertion, drawer opening, cable routing, and object placement under occlusion. Baselines will include pure diffusion policy, diffusion policy with receding-horizon replanning, classical impedance control with scripted references, and residual reinforcement-learning controllers. Metrics will include task success, contact force peaks, recovery from pose perturbations, robustness to visual noise, and generalization to unseen object geometries.\n\nThe expected contribution is a principled framework for treating diffusion policies not as direct low-level controllers, but as probabilistic motion priors constrained by classical feedback control. This could improve reliability in real-world manipulation by combining the expressiveness and multimodality of generative policies with the stability, compliance, and disturbance rejection of established control theory."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/neuroscience] How might chronic social stress alter dopamine signaling in the mesolimbic pathway, and what experimental approach could reveal causal mechanisms?
full_prompt: How might chronic social stress alter dopamine signaling in the mesolimbic pathway, and what experimental approach could reveal causal mechanisms?
completion:
Chronic social stress has a well-developed animal-model literature, so I’ll first look for convergent evidence on mesolimbic dopamine adaptations and causal tools used to test them.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"chronic social defeat stress mesolimbic dopamine VTA nucleus accumbens causal optogenetics fiber photometry"}
```

[result]: Simulated search result: Key papers and reviews point to chronic social defeat stress producing divergent mesolimbic dopamine phenotypes depending on susceptibility vs resilience. Reported changes include increased firing/bursting of ventral tegmental area dopamine neurons projecting to nucleus accumbens, altered dopamine release dynamics in accumbens shell/core, changes in D1/D2 receptor signaling, and modulation by BDNF, CRF, dynorphin/kappa-opioid signaling, and inflammatory pathways. Optogenetic activation of VTA dopamine neurons can promote social avoidance in some paradigms, while projection-specific inhibition or resilience-associated homeostatic adaptations can blunt stress phenotypes.

I’ll read one representative causal study to anchor the proposed mechanism and experimental design. 
```action
{"tool":"read","query":"Chaudhury Walsh Friedman Juarez Ku optogenetic VTA dopamine neurons social defeat stress Nature 2013"}
```

[result]: Simulated read result: Representative finding: brief optogenetic phasic activation of VTA dopamine neurons after subthreshold social defeat can induce social avoidance and depression-like behavior, whereas inhibition of VTA dopamine neurons during social defeat can reduce susceptibility. Related work emphasizes projection specificity: VTA→nucleus accumbens dopamine activity is often linked to susceptibility and aversive salience, while VTA→prefrontal cortex adaptations may differ. These studies support the idea that stress-induced changes in phasic dopamine signaling are not merely correlational but can causally bias social behavior.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"Chronic social stress may alter mesolimbic dopamine signaling by shifting VTA dopamine neurons into a state of enhanced phasic excitability and altered terminal release in the nucleus accumbens, especially in animals that develop social avoidance. Rather than treating dopamine as uniformly “reward-related,” the working hypothesis is that repeated social threat retunes VTA→NAc dopamine to encode aversive salience and defensive motivational states. This could occur through increased intrinsic excitability of VTA dopamine neurons, stress-peptide modulation such as CRF or dynorphin/kappa-opioid signaling, altered BDNF-dependent plasticity in the accumbens, and compensatory changes in D1- versus D2-medium spiny neuron responsiveness.\n\nA causal experimental approach would combine chronic social defeat stress with projection-specific dopamine monitoring and manipulation. Mice would undergo chronic social defeat and then be classified as susceptible or resilient using social interaction behavior. During later social approach, threat-predictive cue exposure, and reward tasks, VTA→NAc dopamine dynamics would be measured with fiber photometry using dLight or GRAB-DA sensors in nucleus accumbens core and shell. This would reveal whether susceptible animals show exaggerated dopamine transients to social threat cues, blunted reward-related transients, or abnormal dopamine clearance/receptor-coupled signaling.\n\nTo test causality, the same behavioral epochs could be paired with closed-loop optogenetic or chemogenetic manipulation of VTA dopamine neurons projecting to NAc. For example, retrograde Cre delivery to NAc combined with Cre-dependent opsins or inhibitory DREADDs in VTA would allow selective control of the VTA→NAc dopamine projection. If phasic stimulation during subthreshold social stress induces later avoidance, and inhibition during defeat prevents avoidance, that would support a causal role for stress-potentiated dopamine bursts. Parallel manipulation of VTA→prefrontal cortex projections would determine whether the effect is specific to the accumbens pathway.\n\nMechanistic resolution could come from ex vivo fast-scan cyclic voltammetry and slice electrophysiology after behavior. These assays would test whether social stress changes dopamine release probability, D2 autoreceptor function, dopamine transporter-mediated clearance, and intrinsic excitability of identified VTA dopamine neurons. Combining these measurements with pharmacological blockade or genetic disruption of candidate mediators such as CRF receptors, kappa-opioid receptors, or BDNF/TrkB signaling would identify molecular routes by which chronic social stress remodels mesolimbic dopamine. The expected outcome is a circuit-level model in which chronic social stress biases VTA→NAc dopamine signaling toward maladaptive threat-salience encoding, producing social avoidance and reduced reward seeking."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Write a free-text research proposal exploring event-triggered control for multi-robot coordination in bandwidth-limited environments, with attention to stabilit
full_prompt: Write a free-text research proposal exploring event-triggered control for multi-robot coordination in bandwidth-limited environments, with attention to stability and scalability.
completion:
To ground the proposal in current robotics-control concerns, I will first search for recent work connecting event-triggered control, multi-agent coordination, communication constraints, and stability guarantees.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"event-triggered control multi-robot coordination bandwidth limited communication stability scalability"}
```

[result]: Recent literature emphasizes event-triggered and self-triggered communication for networked multi-agent systems, especially consensus, formation control, and distributed optimization under bandwidth constraints. Common themes include Lyapunov-based triggering rules, exclusion of Zeno behavior, robustness to delays and packet drops, and scalability challenges when moving from small teams to large robot swarms.

I will read a representative source summary to extract the main technical gaps relevant to a proposal.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"survey event-triggered control multi-agent systems communication constraints Lyapunov Zeno scalability"}
```

[result]: Representative surveys report that event-triggered control can significantly reduce communication load compared with periodic sampling while preserving stability, but open problems remain in heterogeneous robot teams, dynamic interaction graphs, decentralized triggering under unreliable wireless links, and experimental validation at swarm scale. Many methods provide asymptotic or practical stability guarantees, yet scalability often suffers when trigger thresholds require global graph parameters or dense neighbor monitoring.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project proposes to develop scalable event-triggered control methods for coordinating multi-robot systems operating over bandwidth-limited and unreliable communication networks. The central hypothesis is that robots need not communicate periodically to maintain safe and stable coordination; instead, each robot can transmit state, intent, or control-relevant information only when locally measurable events indicate that coordination performance or stability may degrade. The work will focus on formation keeping, rendezvous, and cooperative coverage as representative coordination tasks where communication efficiency and stability are both critical.\n\nThe technical objective is to design decentralized triggering mechanisms that reduce network usage while preserving provable closed-loop guarantees. Each robot will maintain local estimates of neighbor states and trigger transmissions when estimation error, formation error, safety margin, or task-performance residuals exceed adaptive thresholds. Lyapunov and input-to-state stability tools will be used to prove convergence or bounded tracking under intermittent communication, packet loss, delays, and changing interaction graphs. A key requirement will be the exclusion of Zeno behavior, ensuring a positive lower bound on inter-event times so that the controller is implementable on real robots.\n\nA second objective is scalability. Many existing event-triggered coordination methods rely on global network constants, dense neighbor updates, or conservative thresholds that become inefficient as team size grows. This project will investigate local and graph-adaptive triggering rules whose parameters depend only on neighborhood information, estimated connectivity, or task-level urgency. The research will compare static thresholds, adaptive thresholds, and learning-assisted threshold tuning, while retaining formal safety and stability certificates. Particular attention will be given to heterogeneous teams in which robots differ in sensing radius, dynamics, communication range, and onboard computation.\n\nThe methodology will combine theory, simulation, and hardware validation. Theoretical results will be developed for single- and double-integrator robot models, then extended toward nonlinear mobile robot dynamics using passivity or control-Lyapunov formulations. Large-scale simulations will evaluate communication load, convergence rate, robustness, and energy consumption against periodic, time-triggered baselines. Hardware experiments with a small robot fleet will test the approach under realistic wireless congestion, asynchronous clocks, and packet drops.\n\nThe expected outcome is a control framework that enables robot teams to coordinate reliably while using substantially less bandwidth than conventional periodic communication schemes. By unifying event-triggered communication, distributed stability analysis, and scalable multi-robot coordination, the project aims to provide both theoretical guarantees and practical design rules for deploying robot teams in contested, remote, or infrastructure-poor environments where communication is a scarce resource."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Examine control strategies for aerial robots performing contact-rich tasks, such as wall inspection or valve turning, where aerodynamic effects and interaction

completion:
