{"tool":"search","query":"aerial robot contact-rich manipulation wall inspection valve turning interaction force control aerodynamic effects impedance control"}
```

[result]: Recent work clusters around: hybrid position/force control for aerial manipulators; impedance/admittance control for wall contact and inspection; momentum-based external wrench estimation; model predictive control for multirotors near surfaces; and learning-enhanced disturbance compensation for ground effect, wall effect, and rotor–surface interactions. A recurring limitation is that aerodynamic disturbances and contact forces are often treated separately, even though in close-proximity contact tasks they are dynamically coupled.

I’ll read one representative line of work on aerial physical interaction to identify the most useful baseline and likely research gap.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"representative recent papers on aerial physical interaction control, including impedance/MPC-based aerial manipulation and near-wall aerodynamic disturbance compensation"}
```

[result]: Baseline approaches commonly stabilize the vehicle using geometric or NMPC control, regulate interaction through Cartesian impedance or admittance, and estimate contact wrench using force/torque sensors or observer-based methods. Near-wall aerodynamic effects are typically compensated as disturbances or learned residuals, while contact-force objectives are handled in a separate outer loop. Few approaches explicitly co-design contact-force regulation and aerodynamic-effect regulation in a single constrained controller with task-dependent mode transitions.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project proposes a unified control framework for aerial robots performing contact-rich tasks such as wall inspection, pipe contact, and valve turning, where aerodynamic effects and physical interaction forces are jointly regulated rather than treated as independent disturbances. The central hypothesis is that near-surface aerodynamics, rotor-induced flow, and contact wrench dynamics form a coupled interaction system; explicitly modeling or learning this coupling will improve force tracking, attitude stability, and task success during sustained contact.\n\nThe technical approach will combine constrained model predictive control with an interaction-aware disturbance model. The controller will optimize vehicle motion, end-effector force, attitude, and actuator effort while enforcing friction-cone, torque, and safety constraints. A residual model, learned from flight-contact data, will estimate aerodynamic effects such as wall effect, ground effect, and rotor wake interaction as functions of vehicle pose, rotor speed, surface distance, and contact state. This residual will be embedded into the predictive controller alongside a Cartesian impedance layer for compliant interaction.\n\nA second contribution will be a hybrid contact-state manager for transitions between free flight, approach, sliding contact, rigid contact, and manipulation phases such as valve turning. Each mode will use a different balance between motion tracking, force regulation, and aerodynamic compensation. For example, during wall inspection the robot should maintain stable normal force while rejecting lateral flow-induced drift; during valve turning it must regulate end-effector torque while preventing attitude excursions caused by both contact reaction forces and rotor–surface coupling.\n\nThe system will be evaluated in simulation and hardware using a multirotor equipped with a lightweight manipulator or contact probe, force/torque sensing, and onboard state estimation. Experiments will compare the proposed joint regulation strategy against standard impedance control, disturbance-observer compensation, and force-control baselines. Metrics will include contact-force tracking error, pose deviation, energy use, task completion rate, and robustness to different wall materials, surface orientations, and aerodynamic proximity conditions. The expected outcome is a control architecture that enables aerial robots to perform longer-duration, higher-precision physical interaction tasks in cluttered industrial environments."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Write a free-text research proposal on energy-aware control for soft robots, focusing on actuator efficiency, morphology-control co-design, and experimental val
full_prompt: Write a free-text research proposal on energy-aware control for soft robots, focusing on actuator efficiency, morphology-control co-design, and experimental validation.
completion:
I’ll first look for recent themes in energy-aware soft-robot control so the proposal is grounded in current robotics-control language and gaps. Then I’ll synthesize a focused research plan emphasizing actuator efficiency, co-design, and validation.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"energy-aware control soft robots actuator efficiency morphology control co-design experimental validation recent research"}
```

[result]: Recent work highlights (i) model-based and learning-based control for soft actuators under nonlinear hysteresis and fluidic/electrical losses, (ii) morphology-control co-design using differentiable simulation and topology/material optimization, and (iii) growing emphasis on hardware validation because simulation-to-reality gaps remain large for compliant, continuum, pneumatic, hydraulic, dielectric elastomer, and tendon-driven soft robots. Key open gaps include lack of standardized efficiency metrics, weak integration of actuator energetics into control objectives, and few closed-loop experiments showing improved task performance per unit energy across multiple morphologies.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project proposes an energy-aware control framework for soft robots that jointly optimizes actuator use, body morphology, and task performance. Soft robots offer safety and adaptability, but their compliance often comes with poor energetic efficiency due to pneumatic losses, viscoelastic dissipation, hysteresis, and over-actuation. The central hypothesis is that substantial energy savings can be achieved not only by improving low-level control, but by explicitly co-designing morphology and controllers so that passive dynamics, elastic storage, and actuator placement reduce the active work needed for locomotion or manipulation.\n\nThe first research thrust will develop actuator-efficiency models suitable for control. We will characterize representative soft actuation platforms—such as pneumatic networks, tendon-driven elastomeric limbs, and electroactive actuators—using measurements of input energy, mechanical output work, thermal or fluidic losses, response bandwidth, and hysteresis. These models will be embedded into optimal and model-predictive controllers whose objectives include task accuracy, stability, and energy per successful action rather than trajectory tracking alone. Learning-based residual models will compensate for unmodeled nonlinearities while preserving physically meaningful energy terms.\n\nThe second thrust will address morphology-control co-design. We will formulate a differentiable or sampling-based optimization problem over actuator placement, chamber geometry, stiffness distribution, tendon routing, and controller parameters. Instead of treating the soft body as a fixed plant, the method will search for designs that exploit compliance, passive restoring forces, and resonance-like behaviors to reduce control effort. Candidate controllers will include energy-regularized MPC, hybrid learning/model-based policies, and gait or manipulation primitives that exploit elastic energy storage and release. A key outcome will be design rules linking morphology to energetic control authority, such as when added stiffness, segmentation, or underactuation improves efficiency without sacrificing dexterity.\n\nThe third thrust will provide rigorous experimental validation. We will build modular soft-robot testbeds, for example a crawling/undulating soft locomotor and a soft continuum manipulator, with interchangeable actuator layouts and stiffness profiles. Experiments will compare baseline tracking controllers, task-only learning controllers, and the proposed energy-aware co-designed controllers under matched tasks. Metrics will include energy per distance traveled, energy per object manipulation, tracking error, robustness to payload or terrain variation, actuator duty cycle, and thermal/fluidic efficiency. Ablation studies will isolate the contributions of actuator modeling, controller energy regularization, and morphology optimization.\n\nThe expected contribution is a validated methodology for designing soft robots whose intelligence is distributed across materials, morphology, and control. By producing actuator efficiency models, co-design algorithms, and experimentally benchmarked prototypes, the project will advance soft robotics from demonstrations of compliance toward practical systems capable of sustained, efficient operation in field, medical, and human-interactive settings."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/robotics_control] Formulate a research agenda for learning residual dynamics models that augment analytical controllers in high-speed robotic manipulation.
full_prompt: Formulate a research agenda for learning residual dynamics models that augment analytical controllers in high-speed robotic manipulation.
completion:
To ground the agenda, I will first look for recent work at the intersection of residual dynamics learning, model-based control, and high-speed robotic manipulation.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"residual dynamics learning analytical model-based controllers high-speed robotic manipulation robotics"}
```

[result]: Recent literature highlights residual model learning for improving nominal analytical dynamics in model predictive control, LQR/iLQR, and computed-torque control; Gaussian processes, neural state-space models, and differentiable simulators are common residual representations. Related high-speed manipulation work emphasizes contact-rich dynamics, latency, actuator limits, sim-to-real mismatch, and safety constraints, but often treats learned models and analytical controllers separately rather than as a unified co-designed architecture.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"survey/results on residual model learning for robot control and high-speed manipulation"}
```

[result]: Key themes include: residual learning can preserve structure and stability properties of analytical models while compensating for unmodeled friction, impacts, compliance, and aerodynamic or actuator effects; uncertainty-aware residuals are useful for safe MPC and adaptive control; high-speed tasks require real-time inference, excitation-aware data collection, and robustness to distribution shift. Open gaps include stability certification of learned residual-augmented controllers, contact-aware residual representations, and benchmarks for dynamic manipulation.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"A research agenda for learning residual dynamics models that augment analytical controllers in high-speed robotic manipulation should begin from the principle of structured complementarity: analytical models should encode rigid-body dynamics, actuator constraints, known contacts, and controller priors, while learned residual models should focus only on systematic errors that limit performance at high speed. The first thrust should therefore develop residual dynamics architectures that are physically structured, for example by decomposing residuals into frictional, impact, compliance, latency, and actuator-saturation components rather than learning an unconstrained black-box correction. Promising model classes include neural ODE/state-space residuals, Gaussian-process residuals for uncertainty calibration, and hybrid contact-mode residuals that switch or blend across grasping, striking, sliding, and throwing regimes.\n\nA second thrust should study controller-residual co-design. Instead of training a model offline and inserting it into an existing controller, the residual should be learned with the downstream analytical controller in mind: computed-torque control, operational-space control, iLQR, MPC, or passivity-based control. The agenda should investigate stability-preserving residual parameterizations, Lyapunov- or contraction-constrained learning objectives, and uncertainty-aware control laws that reduce reliance on the residual outside its data support. This is especially important in high-speed manipulation, where small model errors can lead to large timing, impact, and contact-force errors.\n\nA third thrust should focus on data generation and adaptation under high-speed constraints. Because aggressive manipulation produces short-duration contact events and rare failure modes, the field needs excitation-aware experiment design, event-triggered sensing, and active learning methods that gather informative data without damaging hardware. Sim-to-real pipelines should use differentiable simulation and domain randomization to initialize residuals, followed by online adaptation from real robot data. Research should also address latency compensation, bandwidth limits, and real-time inference, since a residual model that improves prediction but cannot run inside a millisecond-scale control loop may be unusable.\n\nFinally, the agenda should establish benchmark tasks and evaluation criteria specific to residual-augmented analytical control. Candidate benchmarks include dynamic catching, in-hand regrasping, high-speed pushing, batting, throwing, cable or cloth manipulation, and impact-rich assembly. Metrics should include not only tracking error and task success, but also stability margins, contact prediction accuracy, data efficiency, compute latency, robustness to payload and surface changes, and graceful degradation when the learned residual is uncertain. The long-term goal is a new class of robotic manipulation controllers that retain the interpretability and reliability of analytical control while achieving the adaptability and performance of learned dynamics in regimes where classical models alone are insufficient."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] Write a free-text research proposal to evaluate whether a pharmacist-led medication reconciliation program reduces 30-day hospital readmissions among older adul
full_prompt: Write a free-text research proposal to evaluate whether a pharmacist-led medication reconciliation program reduces 30-day hospital readmissions among older adults with polypharmacy.
completion:
Rationale: I’ll first look for recent evidence on pharmacist-led medication reconciliation and readmissions in older adults to anchor the proposed study design and outcomes.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"pharmacist-led medication reconciliation older adults polypharmacy 30-day hospital readmission randomized trial systematic review"}
```

[result]: Search results suggest that pharmacist-led medication reconciliation and transitional care interventions can reduce medication discrepancies and adverse drug events, but effects on 30-day readmissions are mixed. Evidence is strongest when reconciliation is combined with discharge counseling, communication with primary care, and post-discharge follow-up. Older adults with polypharmacy are frequently identified as a high-risk subgroup likely to benefit from targeted interventions.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This study will evaluate whether a pharmacist-led medication reconciliation program reduces 30-day hospital readmissions among older adults with polypharmacy. We propose a pragmatic, parallel-group randomized controlled trial enrolling hospitalized adults aged 65 years or older who are taking five or more chronic medications at admission and are discharged home or to assisted living. Participants will be randomized before discharge to usual care or to a pharmacist-led medication reconciliation intervention. The primary outcome will be all-cause hospital readmission within 30 days of discharge.\n\nThe intervention will include a structured pharmacist review of pre-admission medications, inpatient medication changes, discharge prescriptions, renal dosing, drug-drug interactions, therapeutic duplication, and high-risk medications. Pharmacists will resolve discrepancies with the inpatient clinical team before discharge, provide patient-centered medication counseling, generate an updated medication list, and communicate medication changes to the patient’s primary care clinician and community pharmacy. A follow-up telephone call within 72 hours of discharge will identify adherence barriers, medication access problems, and emerging adverse effects.\n\nThe control group will receive standard discharge medication reconciliation as performed under existing hospital procedures. Data will be collected from electronic health records, pharmacy documentation, and post-discharge follow-up. Secondary outcomes will include emergency department visits within 30 days, clinically significant medication discrepancies, adverse drug events, medication adherence, patient understanding of medication changes, and mortality. Analyses will follow the intention-to-treat principle, using logistic regression to estimate the effect of the intervention on 30-day readmission while adjusting for baseline readmission risk, comorbidity burden, number of medications, prior hospitalizations, and discharge disposition.\n\nThis trial will address an important gap in transitional care for older adults with polypharmacy, a population at high risk for medication-related harm and preventable readmissions. If the pharmacist-led program reduces readmissions or medication-related complications, it would support broader implementation of pharmacist-integrated discharge workflows. Even if readmissions are not significantly reduced, the study will clarify whether the intervention improves medication safety outcomes that are clinically meaningful for older adults and health systems."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] Draft a free-text research proposal for a randomized controlled trial comparing telehealth-based pulmonary rehabilitation versus standard outpatient rehabilitat
full_prompt: Draft a free-text research proposal for a randomized controlled trial comparing telehealth-based pulmonary rehabilitation versus standard outpatient rehabilitation in patients with moderate to severe COPD.
completion:
To ground the proposal in current evidence, I will first search for recent literature on telehealth pulmonary rehabilitation in COPD and identify key trial design considerations.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"telehealth pulmonary rehabilitation COPD randomized trial moderate severe COPD outpatient rehabilitation outcomes 6-minute walk quality of life"}
```

[result]: Recent studies and systematic reviews suggest that home-based or telehealth pulmonary rehabilitation can improve exercise capacity and health-related quality of life in COPD, but evidence remains heterogeneous due to variation in intervention intensity, supervision, technology platforms, and comparator groups. Common outcomes include 6-minute walk distance, COPD Assessment Test, St George’s Respiratory Questionnaire, dyspnea scores, exacerbations, adherence, and safety events. Several reviews highlight the need for adequately powered non-inferiority or superiority trials directly comparing telehealth-delivered rehabilitation with conventional center-based outpatient pulmonary rehabilitation.

To refine the trial structure, I will read a representative source focused on tele-rehabilitation trial outcomes and methodological gaps.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"systematic review tele-rehabilitation COPD pulmonary rehabilitation outcomes adherence safety randomized controlled trials"}
```

[result]: The review reports that tele-rehabilitation is generally feasible and safe for stable COPD, with adherence often comparable to or better than center-based programs. However, many trials are small, single-center, or use usual care rather than standard outpatient rehabilitation as the comparator. Recommended future trials should include standardized exercise prescriptions, remote monitoring, blinded outcome assessment, longer follow-up, predefined non-inferiority margins for functional outcomes, and assessment of implementation outcomes such as access, adherence, and patient satisfaction.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This study will be a multicenter randomized controlled non-inferiority trial comparing telehealth-based pulmonary rehabilitation with standard outpatient pulmonary rehabilitation in patients with moderate to severe chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD). Eligible participants will be adults aged 40 years or older with spirometry-confirmed COPD, GOLD stage II–IV airflow limitation, clinically stable disease for at least four weeks, and functional limitation warranting pulmonary rehabilitation. Participants will be randomized 1:1 to either an 8-week telehealth pulmonary rehabilitation program or a conventional hospital- or clinic-based outpatient pulmonary rehabilitation program.\n\nThe telehealth intervention will include supervised exercise training delivered by video visits, individualized aerobic and resistance exercise prescriptions, remote symptom and oxygen saturation monitoring when clinically indicated, education on inhaler technique, exacerbation recognition, breathing strategies, nutrition, and self-management. The comparator arm will receive standard outpatient pulmonary rehabilitation of similar duration, frequency, educational content, and exercise intensity, delivered in person according to local best-practice protocols. Both arms will include progression of exercise intensity based on symptoms, perceived exertion, and safety parameters. Outcome assessors will be blinded to group allocation.\n\nThe primary endpoint will be change in 6-minute walk distance from baseline to 8 weeks, with a prespecified non-inferiority margin based on the minimal clinically important difference for COPD. Secondary outcomes will include health-related quality of life measured by the St George’s Respiratory Questionnaire and COPD Assessment Test, dyspnea severity, exercise adherence, program completion, exacerbation frequency, health care utilization, patient satisfaction, and adverse events. Follow-up assessments will occur at baseline, post-intervention at 8 weeks, and at 6 months to evaluate durability of benefit.\n\nThe study hypothesis is that telehealth-based pulmonary rehabilitation will be non-inferior to standard outpatient rehabilitation for improving functional exercise capacity, while improving access and adherence among patients who face transportation, geographic, mobility, or scheduling barriers. Stratified randomization by study site and baseline disease severity will be used, and analyses will follow both intention-to-treat and per-protocol principles, as appropriate for a non-inferiority design. If telehealth rehabilitation achieves comparable clinical outcomes with acceptable safety and higher patient convenience, the findings could support wider implementation of scalable pulmonary rehabilitation models for patients with moderate to severe COPD."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] Develop a study examining the association between continuous glucose monitoring use and hypoglycemia-related emergency visits in adults with type 2 diabetes tre
full_prompt: Develop a study examining the association between continuous glucose monitoring use and hypoglycemia-related emergency visits in adults with type 2 diabetes treated with insulin.
completion:
I’ll first look for recent clinical evidence on CGM use in insulin-treated type 2 diabetes and outcomes related to hypoglycemia or acute care utilization, to ground the proposed study design.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"continuous glucose monitoring insulin-treated type 2 diabetes hypoglycemia emergency department visits cohort study"}
```

[result]: Recent literature includes observational and pragmatic studies suggesting CGM use in adults with insulin-treated type 2 diabetes is associated with improved glycemic control and reduced hypoglycemia. Evidence on severe hypoglycemia and emergency department visits is less definitive, with many studies limited by short follow-up, selected populations, or combined type 1/type 2 diabetes cohorts. Claims and EHR-linked datasets have been used to evaluate acute diabetes-related events before and after CGM initiation.

To refine the design, I’ll review a representative study using real-world data and acute diabetes-related outcomes.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"real-world continuous glucose monitoring type 2 diabetes insulin emergency department hypoglycemia claims"}
```

[result]: A representative real-world study design compared outcomes before and after CGM initiation among insulin-treated adults, often using insurance claims or integrated health system EHR data. Common methods included propensity score matching or inverse probability weighting, adjustment for baseline hypoglycemia history, insulin regimen, comorbidities, HbA1c, diabetes duration proxies, and healthcare utilization. Severe hypoglycemia was typically defined using ICD-coded emergency department or inpatient encounters, though coding sensitivity was a limitation.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This study will examine whether continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) use is associated with reduced hypoglycemia-related emergency department visits among adults with type 2 diabetes treated with insulin. We propose a retrospective cohort study using linked electronic health record and insurance claims data from a large integrated health system or multi-payer claims database. Eligible participants will be adults aged 18 years or older with type 2 diabetes, at least one insulin prescription, continuous health plan enrollment for 12 months before cohort entry, and no prior type 1 diabetes diagnosis. The exposure will be new initiation of real-time or intermittently scanned CGM, identified through device orders, pharmacy claims, or durable medical equipment claims.\n\nThe primary outcome will be hypoglycemia-related emergency department visits within 12 months after cohort entry, defined using validated ICD-10 diagnosis codes for hypoglycemia in the emergency department setting. Secondary outcomes will include hypoglycemia-related hospitalizations, all-cause emergency visits, diabetes-related emergency visits, HbA1c change, and severe hyperglycemia events. CGM initiators will be compared with insulin-treated nonusers who have not initiated CGM by the index date. To reduce confounding by indication, we will use propensity score weighting or matching based on demographics, baseline HbA1c, insulin regimen intensity, prior hypoglycemia, comorbidities, medication use, socioeconomic measures, diabetes complications, and baseline healthcare utilization.\n\nThe primary analysis will estimate adjusted hazard ratios or incidence rate ratios for hypoglycemia-related emergency visits comparing CGM users with matched nonusers over 12 months. Because CGM may be preferentially prescribed to patients at higher hypoglycemia risk, we will conduct sensitivity analyses using a self-controlled pre-post design among CGM initiators, negative control outcomes, and subgroup analyses by insulin regimen, baseline hypoglycemia history, age group, chronic kidney disease status, and baseline HbA1c. We will also assess adherence or persistence with CGM when data are available, distinguishing one-time device acquisition from sustained use.\n\nThis study would address an important clinical and policy question: whether CGM use in insulin-treated type 2 diabetes translates into fewer severe hypoglycemia events requiring emergency care. Findings could inform guideline recommendations, payer coverage decisions, and targeting of CGM to patients most likely to benefit, especially older adults and those with prior hypoglycemia or complex insulin regimens."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] Evaluate the diagnostic accuracy and clinical impact of point-of-care ultrasound for detecting volume overload in patients presenting with acute dyspnea.
full_prompt: Evaluate the diagnostic accuracy and clinical impact of point-of-care ultrasound for detecting volume overload in patients presenting with acute dyspnea.
completion:
Rationale: I will first search for existing evidence on POCUS findings such as lung B-lines, pleural effusions, IVC assessment, and focused cardiac ultrasound in acute dyspnea, then use that to frame a clinically relevant study.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"point-of-care ultrasound volume overload acute dyspnea diagnostic accuracy clinical impact B-lines IVC emergency department"}
```

[result]: Simulated search result: Prior studies and meta-analyses suggest lung ultrasound B-lines have good diagnostic accuracy for acute heart failure in dyspneic patients, often outperforming chest radiography and complementing natriuretic peptides. IVC ultrasound alone shows variable performance due to dependence on respiratory effort, right heart function, and operator technique. Multiorgan POCUS protocols combining lung, cardiac, and IVC views may improve diagnostic confidence and reduce time to treatment, but evidence on patient-centered outcomes such as length of stay, diuretic timing, admission decisions, and readmissions remains less definitive.

Rationale: I will read a representative synthesis to identify gaps that a new proposal should address.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"systematic review lung ultrasound acute heart failure dyspnea emergency department diagnostic accuracy B lines"}
```

[result]: Simulated read result: The review found lung ultrasound to be highly sensitive and moderately-to-highly specific for cardiogenic pulmonary edema in acute dyspnea, particularly when bilateral diffuse B-lines are present. Accuracy improved when interpreted alongside focused echocardiography and clinical assessment. Key limitations included heterogeneity in ultrasound protocols, variable operator training, lack of blinded outcome assessment in some studies, and limited randomized evidence showing that POCUS-guided management improves outcomes beyond diagnostic accuracy.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This study will evaluate both the diagnostic accuracy and clinical impact of point-of-care ultrasound for detecting volume overload among adults presenting to the emergency department with acute dyspnea. The central hypothesis is that a structured multiorgan POCUS protocol—combining lung ultrasound for B-lines and pleural effusions, focused cardiac assessment for ventricular function, and inferior vena cava assessment—will improve early identification of volume overload compared with usual clinical evaluation alone.\n\nWe propose a prospective, multicenter diagnostic accuracy and pragmatic impact study. Consecutive adult patients presenting with undifferentiated acute dyspnea will undergo standardized POCUS within the first hour of evaluation. Treating clinicians will record their pre-POCUS diagnostic impression and management plan, then their post-POCUS impression and plan. The reference standard for volume overload or acute heart failure will be adjudicated by an independent blinded panel using all available clinical data, including natriuretic peptides, imaging, hospital course, response to therapy, and discharge diagnosis, but excluding the study POCUS interpretation when feasible.\n\nThe primary diagnostic outcome will be the sensitivity, specificity, positive predictive value, negative predictive value, and area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of POCUS for adjudicated volume overload. Secondary diagnostic analyses will compare lung ultrasound alone, IVC alone, focused cardiac ultrasound alone, and the combined multiorgan protocol. Prespecified subgroup analyses will examine patients with chronic kidney disease, obesity, COPD, atrial fibrillation, and preserved versus reduced ejection fraction, since these groups often present diagnostic uncertainty.\n\nThe clinical impact component will assess whether POCUS changes management and improves process outcomes. Key endpoints will include change in diagnostic confidence, time to appropriate diuretic therapy, use of chest radiography or CT, hospital admission versus discharge decisions, emergency department length of stay, inpatient length of stay, escalation to noninvasive ventilation, and 30-day revisit or readmission. A stepped-wedge or cluster-randomized implementation phase could further test whether making the protocol available to treating clinicians improves outcomes compared with usual care.\n\nThis study would address an important evidence gap: POCUS is already widely used for acute dyspnea, but its value depends not only on test accuracy but also on whether it improves timely and appropriate care. By evaluating a reproducible multiorgan protocol across diverse emergency departments and linking ultrasound findings to patient-centered outcomes, the project could inform clinical guidelines, training standards, and implementation strategies for POCUS-guided dyspnea evaluation."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] Prepare a free-text research proposal exploring barriers to guideline-directed medical therapy initiation in patients hospitalized with newly diagnosed heart fa
full_prompt: Prepare a free-text research proposal exploring barriers to guideline-directed medical therapy initiation in patients hospitalized with newly diagnosed heart failure with reduced ejection fraction.
completion:
I’ll first identify current evidence on inpatient initiation of guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) for newly diagnosed HFrEF and common implementation barriers, then use that to frame a focused clinical research proposal.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"barriers to initiation of guideline-directed medical therapy hospitalized newly diagnosed heart failure reduced ejection fraction inpatient GDMT initiation"}
```

[result]: Recent reviews and implementation studies highlight that hospitalized HFrEF patients frequently leave the hospital without full initiation of the four foundational GDMT classes: ARNI/ACEi/ARB, evidence-based beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and SGLT2 inhibitor. Reported barriers include hypotension, renal dysfunction, hyperkalemia, clinician concern about medication tolerability during acute decompensation, therapeutic inertia, fragmented inpatient-to-outpatient transitions, lack of standardized order sets, medication affordability, and limited cardiology involvement. Trials and consensus pathways increasingly support early inpatient initiation when clinically stable.

I’ll read a representative guideline or consensus source to ensure the proposal aligns with contemporary clinical standards.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"2022 AHA ACC HFSA guideline heart failure GDMT initiation hospitalization HFrEF"}
```

[result]: The 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA heart failure guideline recommends four foundational medication classes for HFrEF: renin-angiotensin system inhibition, preferably ARNI when feasible; evidence-based beta-blockers; mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists; and SGLT2 inhibitors. The guideline emphasizes optimizing GDMT to reduce mortality and hospitalization. Subsequent expert pathways encourage initiation or rapid sequencing during hospitalization or soon after discharge once patients are hemodynamically stable, with attention to renal function, potassium, blood pressure, cost, and follow-up.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This study proposes a mixed-methods investigation of barriers to guideline-directed medical therapy initiation among adults hospitalized with newly diagnosed heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF). Although contemporary guidelines recommend early initiation of four foundational therapies—ARNI/ACEi/ARB, evidence-based beta-blocker, mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and SGLT2 inhibitor—many patients are discharged on incomplete regimens. Hospitalization for new HFrEF represents a high-yield opportunity to initiate therapy, educate patients, and establish a longitudinal care plan, yet real-world uptake remains inconsistent.\n\nWe will conduct a retrospective cohort study of patients admitted to a large academic health system with newly diagnosed HFrEF, defined by left ventricular ejection fraction ≤40% and no prior documented HFrEF diagnosis. The primary outcome will be initiation of each GDMT class before discharge, with a secondary composite outcome of discharge on all clinically eligible foundational therapies. Patient-level predictors will include age, sex, race/ethnicity, insurance status, comorbid kidney disease, blood pressure, potassium, estimated glomerular filtration rate, intensive care requirement, length of stay, cardiology consultation, discharge service, and medication cost indicators. Multivariable logistic regression will identify factors independently associated with non-initiation after accounting for documented contraindications.\n\nTo complement quantitative findings, we will perform semi-structured interviews with clinicians involved in inpatient HFrEF care, including hospitalists, cardiologists, advanced practice clinicians, pharmacists, nurses, and discharge coordinators. Interviews will explore perceived barriers such as concerns about hypotension or renal dysfunction, uncertainty about timing during acute decompensation, role ambiguity between inpatient and outpatient clinicians, formulary and prior authorization challenges, limited discharge follow-up, and patient affordability or health literacy. A smaller patient interview cohort will examine understanding of the new diagnosis, medication concerns, anticipated adherence barriers, and experiences with discharge counseling.\n\nThe expected outcome is a practical taxonomy of modifiable barriers to inpatient GDMT initiation in newly diagnosed HFrEF. Findings will inform development of an implementation intervention, such as an electronic health record–embedded eligibility checklist, pharmacist-supported GDMT initiation protocol, standardized discharge pathway, and early post-discharge titration clinic referral. By identifying where and why eligible patients fail to receive foundational therapy, this study aims to support more reliable early treatment, reduce inequities in HFrEF care, and improve downstream outcomes including readmission, mortality, and quality of life."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] Investigate whether early palliative care consultation improves symptom burden, quality of life, and healthcare utilization among patients newly diagnosed with
full_prompt: Investigate whether early palliative care consultation improves symptom burden, quality of life, and healthcare utilization among patients newly diagnosed with advanced heart failure.
completion:
A focused literature search should identify the strongest randomized and pragmatic studies of palliative care in advanced heart failure, plus gaps specific to “newly diagnosed” advanced disease. I will search for trials and systematic reviews on early palliative care consultation in advanced heart failure outcomes.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"early palliative care consultation advanced heart failure newly diagnosed symptom burden quality of life healthcare utilization randomized trial PAL-HF ENABLE CHF-PC systematic review"}
```  
[result]: Found key sources: (1) Rogers et al., “Palliative Care in Heart Failure: The PAL-HF Randomized Clinical Trial,” JACC 2017—interdisciplinary palliative care improved Kansas City Cardiomyopathy Questionnaire and FACIT-Pal scores, anxiety/depression, spiritual well-being; no clear mortality signal. (2) Sidebottom et al., “Inpatient palliative care for patients with acute heart failure,” J Palliat Med 2015—consultation associated with improved symptom documentation/goals-of-care and possible utilization effects. (3) ENABLE CHF-PC pilot RCT—telehealth nurse-led early palliative care in advanced HF showed feasibility and signals for quality-of-life/mood benefit, with mixed utilization outcomes. (4) Systematic reviews report consistent improvement in quality of life and patient-centered outcomes but heterogeneous effects on hospitalization, emergency department use, and costs; most studies enroll prevalent advanced HF or hospitalized patients rather than at time of new advanced-HF diagnosis.

To ground the proposal, I will read the most relevant randomized trial to clarify intervention components, outcomes, and limitations for a newly diagnosed advanced-HF cohort.  
```action
{"tool":"read","query":"Rogers PAL-HF randomized clinical trial palliative care heart failure intervention outcomes limitations"}
```  
[result]: PAL-HF randomized 150 patients with advanced HF and high predicted mortality to usual care versus usual care plus an interdisciplinary palliative care intervention. The intervention included assessment and management of symptoms, psychosocial/spiritual concerns, advance care planning, caregiver support, and care coordination. Compared with usual care, patients receiving palliative care had significantly better disease-specific and palliative-care quality of life and less anxiety/depression at 6 months. The trial was single-center, relatively small, and focused on high-risk advanced HF rather than systematic consultation at the moment of new advanced-HF diagnosis; utilization outcomes were not the primary focus and remained uncertain.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"I propose a multicenter pragmatic randomized controlled trial testing whether automatic early palliative care consultation at the time of newly diagnosed advanced heart failure improves patient-centered outcomes and reduces high-intensity healthcare utilization. Eligible patients would be adults newly meeting advanced heart failure criteria—NYHA class III–IV symptoms despite guideline-directed therapy, recent heart-failure hospitalization, inotrope consideration, left ventricular assist device/transplant evaluation, or clinician-estimated high 1-year mortality—within 60 days of recognition by cardiology or hospital medicine. Participants would be randomized to usual advanced-HF care versus usual care plus structured palliative care consultation initiated within 2 weeks, with follow-up visits at 1, 3, and 6 months and as clinically needed.\n\nThe intervention would be interdisciplinary and standardized but flexible: symptom assessment and management, medication-burden review, psychosocial and spiritual screening, caregiver needs assessment, prognostic understanding, advance care planning, and coordination with cardiology regarding device therapy, transplant/LVAD candidacy, and decompensation plans. This design builds on trials such as PAL-HF, which showed improved quality of life and mood in advanced heart failure, while addressing a key gap: whether consultation introduced immediately after advanced-HF recognition changes symptom trajectory and utilization before repeated hospitalizations occur.\n\nThe primary outcome would be change in Kansas City Cardiomyopathy Questionnaire overall summary score at 6 months. Secondary outcomes would include Edmonton Symptom Assessment System score, PROMIS depression/anxiety, FACIT-Pal or similar palliative-care quality-of-life measure, caregiver burden, completion of documented goals-of-care discussions, hospitalization days, emergency department visits, ICU admissions, 30-day readmissions, hospice enrollment timing, total healthcare costs, and survival. Outcomes would be assessed by blinded research staff using intention-to-treat analysis, with prespecified subgroup analyses by reduced versus preserved ejection fraction, inpatient versus outpatient diagnosis, and eligibility for advanced therapies.\n\nThe central hypothesis is that early palliative care will reduce symptom burden and improve quality of life by aligning treatment with patient priorities, improving symptom control, and supporting caregivers, while also decreasing avoidable acute-care use through anticipatory planning. Because prior evidence suggests strong quality-of-life benefit but mixed utilization effects, the trial should be powered primarily for patient-reported outcomes and secondarily for utilization, with embedded process evaluation to identify mechanisms such as advance care planning, medication optimization, and crisis planning. If effective, the study would support automatic palliative care referral as a standard component of advanced heart-failure care at diagnosis rather than reserving consultation for terminal decline."}
```יישׁ

Deployment barriers include consumer trust, interoperability with home devices, cybersecurity risks, regulatory uncertainty, model robustness, comfort constraints, and utility program design.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project proposes to investigate how artificial intelligence can optimize residential demand response while preserving consumer privacy. The central research question is: can privacy-preserving AI systems deliver demand reductions comparable to centralized optimization while limiting the disclosure of household behavior? The study will focus on residential flexible loads such as HVAC systems, water heaters, batteries, electric vehicles, and smart appliances, with the goal of reducing peak demand, improving renewable integration, and maintaining occupant comfort.\n\nThe project will require several categories of data: smart-meter or submetered electricity consumption, weather forecasts, electricity prices or demand response event signals, building characteristics, controllable device states, and comfort-related variables such as indoor temperature. Because high-resolution energy data can reveal occupancy and lifestyle patterns, the study will compare three data architectures: centralized learning, federated learning in which models are trained locally in homes, and edge-based control where raw household data never leaves the residence. Privacy protections such as differential privacy, secure aggregation, and data minimization will be evaluated alongside performance metrics.\n\nThe experimental design will combine simulation and field validation. First, a representative set of residential buildings will be modeled using historical load, weather, and tariff data. AI controllers, including reinforcement learning and model predictive control, will be tested against baseline thermostat schedules and conventional utility demand response rules. Second, a pilot study with volunteer households or a living-lab testbed will evaluate real-world performance. Outcomes will include peak load reduction, total energy cost savings, rebound effects, occupant comfort, communication requirements, and formal privacy leakage metrics such as the ability to infer occupancy from shared data.\n\nPotential barriers to deployment include consumer distrust, lack of interoperability among smart devices, cybersecurity vulnerabilities, regulatory limits on data sharing, and uneven access to smart technologies. Utilities may also face challenges integrating AI-based household control into existing demand response programs, particularly if models are difficult to explain or verify. The project will therefore produce not only technical results, but also deployment guidelines covering consent, transparency, privacy-by-design architecture, incentive design, and standards for secure communication between homes, aggregators, and utilities."}

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/energy] Investigate the lifecycle emissions and cost tradeoffs of green hydrogen production using offshore wind power compared with grid-connected electrolysis.
full_prompt: Investigate the lifecycle emissions and cost tradeoffs of green hydrogen production using offshore wind power compared with grid-connected electrolysis.
completion:
Rationale: I will first scan for recent evidence on lifecycle emissions and cost drivers for offshore-wind-powered hydrogen versus grid-connected electrolysis, then use one targeted source to ground the proposal.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"lifecycle emissions cost green hydrogen offshore wind electrolysis versus grid connected electrolysis LCOH carbon intensity"}
```

[result]: Recent literature and reports highlight that lifecycle hydrogen emissions depend strongly on electricity carbon intensity, electrolyzer utilization, embodied emissions of offshore wind and electrolyzer equipment, and temporal matching rules. Offshore-wind-to-hydrogen studies often estimate low lifecycle emissions but higher hydrogen costs due to offshore wind capital costs, transmission/offshore platform costs, and lower capacity factors than fully grid-optimized operation. Grid-connected electrolysis can reduce costs by operating during low-price hours, but emissions vary widely depending on marginal grid generation and whether renewable energy certificates, power purchase agreements, or hourly matching are used.

Rationale: I will read a focused source on hydrogen lifecycle accounting and grid electricity attribution to shape the comparison framework.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"hydrogen lifecycle emissions electrolysis grid electricity hourly matching marginal emissions offshore wind"}
```

[result]: Source synthesis: Lifecycle assessment frameworks increasingly distinguish average grid emissions, marginal grid emissions, and contract-based renewable procurement. Studies warn that annual matching can understate emissions if electrolyzers consume fossil-heavy power during some hours, while hourly matching to zero-carbon generation gives lower but potentially more expensive hydrogen due to reduced utilization. Offshore wind dedicated electrolysis tends to offer clearer additionality and low operational emissions, but its cost depends on whether electrolysis is onshore or offshore, curtailment, desalination needs, storage, pipeline/export infrastructure, and electrolyzer capacity sizing relative to wind output.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will evaluate the lifecycle emissions and cost tradeoffs of green hydrogen produced from dedicated offshore wind power compared with grid-connected electrolysis. The central research question is: under what electricity-market, technology-cost, and lifecycle-accounting assumptions does offshore-wind hydrogen deliver lower emissions, and when are those emissions benefits outweighed by higher hydrogen costs? The study will compare three archetypes: dedicated offshore wind with onshore electrolysis, dedicated offshore wind with offshore electrolysis, and grid-connected electrolysis using alternative procurement rules such as no matching, annual renewable matching, and hourly clean-electricity matching.\n\nThe methodology will combine lifecycle assessment with techno-economic optimization. The lifecycle model will include embodied emissions from wind turbines, foundations, subsea cables, electrolyzers, desalination, compression, storage, and hydrogen transport, as well as operational electricity emissions. For grid-connected electrolysis, emissions will be estimated using both average and marginal hourly grid emission factors to test the sensitivity of results to accounting method. The cost model will estimate levelized cost of hydrogen by varying offshore wind capital costs, electrolyzer capital costs, capacity factor, electricity prices, curtailment, financing assumptions, and storage requirements.\n\nA key contribution will be to identify the emissions-cost frontier rather than treating “green hydrogen” as a single category. Dedicated offshore wind is expected to have consistently low lifecycle emissions and strong additionality, but may face higher costs from offshore infrastructure and lower electrolyzer utilization unless systems are optimally sized. Grid-connected electrolysis may achieve lower costs by using cheap grid power and high utilization, but its true emissions advantage will depend on hourly grid conditions and whether clean power procurement is physically and temporally matched to consumption.\n\nThe expected outcome is a decision framework for policymakers, hydrogen developers, and energy-system planners. The study will produce benchmark ranges for grams CO2-equivalent per kilogram of hydrogen and dollars per kilogram of hydrogen across scenarios, plus sensitivity analysis showing which variables most influence the tradeoff. The results can inform subsidy design, clean hydrogen certification rules, offshore wind leasing strategy, and infrastructure planning by clarifying when offshore-wind hydrogen provides genuine lifecycle emissions benefits relative to grid-connected alternatives."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/energy] Write a free-text research proposal on how long-duration energy storage could support grid reliability in regions with high solar and wind penetration. Include
full_prompt: Write a free-text research proposal on how long-duration energy storage could support grid reliability in regions with high solar and wind penetration. Include the key research question, methods, expected outcomes, and policy relevance.
completion:
I’ll first scan for recent evidence on long-duration energy storage (LDES), grid reliability, and high variable renewable energy penetration to ground the proposal in current research and policy needs.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"long duration energy storage grid reliability high wind solar penetration resource adequacy policy research 2024"}
```

[result]: Search results indicate strong current interest in LDES for systems with high variable renewable energy (VRE), especially for multi-day renewable droughts, seasonal mismatch, resource adequacy, transmission congestion relief, and reducing reliance on gas peakers. Recent reports from DOE, NREL, IEA, and grid operators emphasize needs for improved capacity accreditation, co-optimization with transmission and demand response, and valuation of reliability services beyond arbitrage.  

To shape a focused proposal, I’ll read a representative synthesis on LDES valuation and reliability contributions.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"DOE NREL long duration energy storage reliability valuation high renewable grid"}
```

[result]: The synthesis highlights that LDES technologies—such as pumped hydropower, compressed-air energy storage, flow batteries, thermal storage, hydrogen, and emerging electrochemical systems—can provide energy shifting over 10+ hours to multiple days. Key gaps include quantifying reliability value under weather-correlated renewable shortfalls, modeling technology-specific operational constraints, and designing market rules/capacity accreditation methods that compensate firm clean capacity and resilience benefits.  

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project asks: under what technical, economic, and policy conditions can long-duration energy storage improve grid reliability in regions with high solar and wind penetration? As power systems shift toward variable renewable energy, reliability risks increasingly arise not only from peak demand but from extended periods of low wind and solar output, transmission constraints, and weather-correlated supply shortfalls. The research will evaluate how LDES can contribute to resource adequacy, operating reserves, congestion management, and resilience during multi-hour to multi-day renewable droughts.\n\nThe study will use a mixed-methods design combining power-system modeling, scenario analysis, and policy evaluation. First, it will develop regional case studies for grids with high renewable penetration, such as California, Texas, and selected European or island systems. A capacity-expansion and production-cost modeling framework will compare portfolios with different LDES technologies, including pumped hydro, compressed air, flow batteries, thermal storage, and hydrogen-based storage. Scenarios will vary renewable penetration, electrification-driven demand growth, fuel prices, transmission buildout, weather years, and storage duration from 8 hours to multiple days.\n\nThe empirical analysis will focus on reliability metrics such as loss-of-load expectation, expected unserved energy, effective load-carrying capability, ramping sufficiency, curtailment reduction, and dependence on fossil backup generation. The project will also examine operational behavior during extreme events, including heat waves, winter storms, and multi-day low-renewable periods. Sensitivity analysis will identify when LDES provides the greatest marginal reliability value compared with alternatives such as short-duration batteries, demand response, firm low-carbon generation, and new transmission.\n\nExpected outcomes include a quantitative estimate of the reliability contribution of LDES across different renewable-grid conditions, a typology of which storage durations and technologies are best suited to specific reliability challenges, and practical guidance for planners on how to incorporate LDES into resource adequacy studies. The research is expected to show that LDES is most valuable where renewable output is highly correlated across large regions, where transmission expansion is constrained, and where decarbonization policies limit reliance on gas-fired peaking plants.\n\nThe policy relevance is direct. Current electricity markets often reward short-term energy arbitrage but undervalue multi-day reliability, resilience, and firm clean capacity. The project will therefore assess reforms to capacity accreditation, clean firm procurement, ancillary-service markets, and integrated resource planning rules. Its findings can help regulators, system operators, and energy ministries design technology-neutral policies that compensate LDES for reliability services, accelerate investment in appropriate storage durations, and support reliable decarbonization of power systems with high solar and wind penetration."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/clinical_medicine] Write a free-text research proposal assessing the effectiveness of an emergency department clinical decision support tool for improving timely recognition and t
full_prompt: Write a free-text research proposal assessing the effectiveness of an emergency department clinical decision support tool for improving timely recognition and treatment of sepsis.
completion:
I’ll ground the proposal in current sepsis quality priorities and known clinical decision support challenges, then turn that into a pragmatic evaluation plan.  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"emergency department sepsis clinical decision support timely recognition treatment evaluation outcomes alert fatigue SEP-1"}
```
[result]: Recent literature and policy discussions emphasize that ED sepsis CDS tools aim to shorten time to recognition, lactate measurement, blood cultures, antibiotics, fluids, and SEP-1 bundle completion. Reported effectiveness is mixed: some alert-based interventions improve screening and process measures, while others show limited mortality benefit or create alert fatigue, particularly when specificity is low. Recommended evaluations include interrupted time series or stepped-wedge/pragmatic designs, adjustment for secular trends, monitoring false positives, clinician workflow impact, and balancing measures such as unnecessary antibiotics and broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure.

To refine the proposal, I’ll read a representative source on best practices and pitfalls in sepsis CDS implementation and evaluation.  
```action
{"tool":"read","query":"representative review sepsis clinical decision support emergency department implementation evaluation alert fatigue outcomes"}
```
[result]: The reviewed source highlights that successful sepsis CDS depends on integration into ED workflow, actionable recommendations rather than passive alerts, local calibration of prediction rules, clinician trust, and continuous feedback. Evaluation should combine quantitative outcomes—time to antibiotics, lactate testing, bundle adherence, ICU transfer, mortality, length of stay—with qualitative assessment of usability, override reasons, and unintended consequences. Strong designs should distinguish true earlier recognition from changes in documentation or coding.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This study will assess whether an emergency department clinical decision support tool improves timely recognition and treatment of sepsis among adult patients presenting to a large acute-care hospital network. The intervention is an electronic health record–integrated CDS that continuously screens triage data, vital signs, laboratory results, comorbidities, and clinician-entered symptoms to identify patients at risk for sepsis or septic shock. When triggered, the tool notifies the treating team and provides an actionable order set for lactate measurement, blood cultures, early broad-spectrum antibiotics, intravenous fluids when indicated, reassessment prompts, and escalation guidance. The central hypothesis is that an actionable, workflow-integrated CDS will reduce time to sepsis recognition and evidence-based treatment without substantially increasing unnecessary antibiotic use or alert burden.\n\nWe propose a pragmatic stepped-wedge cluster trial across multiple emergency departments, with sites crossing from usual care to CDS implementation at randomly assigned intervals. Eligible encounters will include adults evaluated in the ED who meet Sepsis-3 clinical criteria during the ED stay or within 6 hours of admission, with prespecified subgroup analyses for septic shock, older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients presenting with atypical symptoms. The primary effectiveness outcome will be time from ED arrival to sepsis recognition, defined by clinician documentation, sepsis order-set activation, or initiation of sepsis-directed treatment. Key secondary outcomes will include time to lactate testing, time to blood cultures, time to first appropriate antibiotic, completion of recommended sepsis bundle elements, ICU admission, hospital length of stay, in-hospital mortality, and 30-day mortality.\n\nBecause sepsis CDS tools may improve process metrics while also producing false positives, alert fatigue, or antibiotic overuse, the study will include predefined balancing and implementation outcomes. These will include alert frequency per clinician shift, positive predictive value of alerts, override rates and reasons, broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure among patients ultimately not meeting sepsis criteria, Clostridioides difficile infection, acute kidney injury potentially related to treatment, and clinician-reported usability. Quantitative analyses will use mixed-effects regression models accounting for clustering by site, time period, patient severity, comorbidity burden, crowding, and seasonal infection trends. An interrupted time-series sensitivity analysis will test whether observed changes reflect the CDS rather than secular quality-improvement trends or documentation shifts.\n\nA complementary mixed-methods component will explore how clinicians use the tool in real ED workflow. Brief surveys and semi-structured interviews with physicians, advanced practice clinicians, nurses, pharmacists, and informatics staff will assess perceived usefulness, trust, cognitive workload, barriers to response, and suggestions for refinement. Chart review of a stratified sample of true-positive, false-positive, and missed sepsis cases will identify failure modes, including delayed triage data entry, atypical presentations, competing emergencies, or inappropriate alert thresholds.\n\nThe expected contribution is a rigorous, clinically meaningful assessment of whether ED sepsis CDS improves early recognition and treatment while preserving diagnostic stewardship and clinician workflow. If effective, the study will provide evidence for scalable implementation, including optimal alert thresholds, order-set design, feedback mechanisms, and monitoring metrics. If effectiveness is limited, the findings will clarify whether shortcomings arise from prediction accuracy, workflow integration, clinician response, or unintended consequences, thereby guiding safer and more targeted next-generation sepsis decision support."}
```

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This study will assess the role of advanced nuclear reactors in decarbonizing industrial heat demand through an integrated techno-economic, safety, infrastructure, and public acceptance framework. The central research question is: under what industrial, regional, and policy conditions can advanced reactors provide competitive, safe, and socially acceptable low-carbon heat compared with alternatives such as electrification, hydrogen, biomass, carbon capture, and thermal storage? The study will focus on four industrial sectors with different heat profiles: chemicals, refining, steel, and pulp and paper.\n\nThe technical and economic analysis will model several reactor options, including small modular light-water reactors, high-temperature gas reactors, molten salt reactors, and microreactors. Each will be matched to industrial heat requirements by temperature, capacity factor, reliability needs, and site configuration. The study will estimate levelized cost of heat, cogenerated electricity value, emissions reductions, construction and financing risk, fuel cycle costs, and sensitivity to carbon prices, natural gas prices, learning rates, and regulatory timelines. Particular attention will be given to first-of-a-kind versus nth-of-a-kind cost assumptions, since economics may differ sharply between early demonstration and commercial deployment.\n\nSafety will be examined through a comparative risk framework covering passive safety features, accident scenarios, emergency planning zones, industrial co-location hazards, cybersecurity, fuel handling, waste management, and regulatory readiness. The study will not treat safety only as a technical parameter; it will also evaluate how safety claims are communicated to workers, host communities, regulators, and industrial customers. Case studies of potential deployment sites will assess whether advanced reactor safety characteristics reduce siting constraints relative to conventional nuclear plants, and whether additional risks arise from coupling reactors directly to industrial facilities.\n\nPublic acceptance will be studied through stakeholder interviews, community surveys, and deliberative workshops in regions with existing industrial clusters and energy infrastructure. The research will test how acceptance varies with local economic benefits, ownership models, emergency preparedness, waste-management plans, labor impacts, and trust in institutions. It will also examine whether advanced nuclear is viewed differently when framed as industrial decarbonization infrastructure rather than grid electricity generation.\n\nFinally, the project will analyze integration with existing energy systems, including steam networks, district heating, hydrogen production, electric grids, thermal storage, and retired fossil plant sites. The expected output is a decision-support framework identifying where advanced reactors are likely to be technically feasible, economically competitive, safe to deploy, and socially legitimate. The study will provide policymakers and industrial firms with deployment pathways, risk-mitigation strategies, and criteria for prioritizing demonstration projects in hard-to-abate industrial sectors."}

completion:

```action

```

[result]: Recent studies link energy poverty to constrained adoption of clean energy technologies through affordability barriers, weak infrastructure, limited credit access, and low trust in new systems. Evidence suggests rural households often face higher upfront costs, weaker grid reliability, and poorer access to installers or maintenance services, while urban low-income households may experience energy poverty through high energy burdens, insecure housing tenure, and limited control over building-level energy systems. Clean cooking, rooftop solar, efficient appliances, and mini-grid or off-grid systems are common technologies examined.

To sharpen the proposal, I’ll read one representative synthesis on mechanisms of adoption and barriers across settlement types.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"review article energy poverty barriers clean energy technology adoption households"}
```

[result]: A review of household clean energy adoption identifies four recurring determinants: economic capacity, infrastructure availability, institutional support, and socio-cultural acceptance. Energy-poor households are less likely to adopt clean technologies when upfront costs are high, payback periods are uncertain, subsidies are difficult to access, or technologies do not match daily practices. The review emphasizes that adoption is not a single decision but a process involving awareness, acquisition, sustained use, maintenance, and possible stacking with older fuels or technologies.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This study proposes to examine how energy poverty shapes household adoption of clean energy technologies across urban and rural communities, with attention to both initial uptake and sustained use. The central research question is: how do different dimensions of energy poverty—affordability, reliability, access, housing conditions, and energy burden—influence household decisions to adopt technologies such as clean cooking systems, rooftop solar, solar home systems, efficient appliances, and mini-grid connections?\n\nThe project will use a comparative mixed-methods design. Quantitative household surveys will measure energy expenditure, income, outage frequency, fuel use, technology ownership, access to credit or subsidies, and perceived reliability of existing energy services. These data will be compared across urban low-income neighborhoods and rural communities to identify whether energy poverty operates differently by location. In urban areas, the study will examine constraints such as rented housing, landlord control, high electricity bills, and limited space for household-scale technologies. In rural areas, it will assess barriers such as weak grid extension, distance from markets, lack of maintenance services, and dependence on biomass or diesel.\n\nQualitative interviews and focus groups will investigate why households accept, reject, or discontinue clean energy technologies. This component is important because adoption is not only an economic decision; it is also shaped by trust, gendered labor patterns, cooking preferences, perceptions of technology performance, and experiences with government or private-sector programs. The study will also explore whether households engage in “energy stacking,” combining clean technologies with traditional fuels because clean options are unaffordable, unreliable, or culturally unsuitable.\n\nThe expected contribution is to show that energy poverty can both suppress and motivate clean energy adoption. Severe deprivation may increase demand for reliable and affordable alternatives, but it can also prevent adoption when households lack savings, credit, secure tenure, or technical support. By comparing urban and rural communities, the research will generate policy recommendations tailored to local conditions, such as targeted subsidies, pay-as-you-go financing, tenant-inclusive energy programs, rural maintenance networks, and community-based awareness campaigns. Ultimately, the study aims to inform energy policies that treat clean energy adoption not merely as a technology diffusion problem, but as a justice-centered response to unequal energy access."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/energy] Assess the technical and economic feasibility of repurposing retired coal power plant sites for renewable energy generation, battery storage, or grid-support se
full_prompt: Assess the technical and economic feasibility of repurposing retired coal power plant sites for renewable energy generation, battery storage, or grid-support services.
completion:
I’ll ground the assessment in recent evidence on coal-site redevelopment advantages—interconnection rights, land, transmission access, environmental constraints, and replacement-resource economics—then synthesize a feasibility-focused research plan.
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"retired coal power plant site redevelopment renewable energy battery storage grid services interconnection transmission feasibility economics"}
```

[result]: Search results indicate that retired coal plant sites are increasingly considered attractive for solar PV, wind where resource permits, battery energy storage systems (BESS), synchronous condensers, and other grid-support assets. Key advantages include existing transmission interconnection, substations, roads, water access, industrial zoning, and local workforce familiarity. Major constraints include remediation liabilities, cooling-water and ash-pond cleanup, uncertain ownership of interconnection rights, declining suitability of some sites for large-scale renewables due to land limits, and market dependence on capacity, ancillary-service, and energy-arbitrage revenues. Studies from NREL, DOE, IEA, and utility integrated resource plans commonly find that BESS and grid-support reuse can be technically easier than full renewable generation reuse, while hybrid solar-plus-storage can be attractive where land, irradiance, and interconnection capacity align.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"DOE/NREL reports and utility case studies on coal plant redevelopment, storage siting, and transmission interconnection reuse"}
```

[result]: Evidence from case studies suggests that the strongest redevelopment candidates are coal sites with high-voltage interconnection capacity, available land outside contaminated areas, clear title and permitting pathways, and proximity to load or congestion zones. Repowering options include: utility-scale solar PV on buffer land or remediated ash areas; wind only in favorable regions and usually with off-site footprints; lithium-ion BESS using existing switchyards; long-duration storage where policy incentives exist; synchronous condensers or grid-forming inverters to replace inertia, voltage support, and short-circuit strength lost after coal retirement. Economically, avoided transmission-upgrade costs and faster interconnection can be decisive, but remediation costs, tax treatment, market rules, and community transition benefits materially affect project viability.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This research will assess the technical and economic feasibility of repurposing retired coal power plant sites for renewable generation, battery storage, and grid-support services. The central hypothesis is that coal-site reuse is often most feasible where the project monetizes existing interconnection, substation infrastructure, industrial land use, and local grid-service needs, but that feasibility varies sharply by site remediation burden, land availability, renewable resource quality, and wholesale-market design.\n\nTechnically, the study should classify candidate sites by reuse pathway: solar PV, wind, solar-plus-storage, standalone battery storage, long-duration storage, synchronous condensers, grid-forming inverter systems, or hybrid configurations. For each site, the analysis would evaluate interconnection capacity, transmission constraints, land area, contamination and ash-pond limitations, geotechnical suitability, renewable resource quality, cooling-water or industrial infrastructure reuse, and the ability to provide reliability services such as voltage support, frequency response, black start, inertia replacement, and congestion relief.\n\nEconomically, the project should compare levelized cost, net present value, payback period, and system value across reuse options. A key part of the assessment would be quantifying avoided costs: reduced interconnection delays, avoided greenfield transmission upgrades, lower land-acquisition costs, and potential use of existing roads, substations, control buildings, and grid rights. Revenue modeling should include energy sales, capacity payments, ancillary services, transmission deferral value, congestion rents, tax credits, remediation costs, decommissioning liabilities, and possible state or federal transition incentives.\n\nThe expected finding is that standalone BESS and grid-support conversions are likely to be technically feasible at the widest range of retired coal sites because they require relatively little land and can directly use existing high-voltage infrastructure. Solar-plus-storage will be highly feasible where sufficient uncontaminated or remediated land is available and interconnection capacity remains valuable. Wind reuse will be more site-specific, often requiring nearby rather than on-site development. Overall, coal-site repurposing can be economically attractive, but only when redevelopment planning integrates environmental cleanup, interconnection rights, market revenues, and community-transition objectives from the outset."}
```
