It notes that attrition and differential follow-up are major biases and recommends multilingual recruitment, hybrid remote/in-person visits, compensation, and inverse probability weighting for loss to follow-up.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"We propose a multicenter prospective cohort study of adults and adolescents with laboratory-confirmed acute SARS-CoV-2 infection to identify clinical, laboratory, and social determinants of long COVID symptom persistence at 6 and 12 months. Participants will be enrolled within 14 days of positive PCR or antigen testing across outpatient testing sites, primary care practices, emergency departments, and hospitals to capture the full severity spectrum. A comparison group with contemporaneous test-negative respiratory illness may also be recruited to distinguish post-COVID symptoms from background post-viral or pandemic-related symptoms. The primary outcome will be persistent long COVID symptoms at 6 and 12 months, defined using WHO-compatible criteria plus standardized symptom and functional impairment instruments.\n\nBaseline data will include demographics, vaccination history, prior SARS-CoV-2 infection, variant period, comorbidities, medications, pregnancy status, smoking, BMI, acute symptom burden, hospitalization, oxygen requirement, and treatments received. Laboratory assessment during acute infection will measure complete blood count with differential, CRP, ferritin, D-dimer, liver and kidney function, HbA1c, SARS-CoV-2 viral load or cycle threshold when available, serology, and a biobank panel for cytokines, autoantibodies, immune cell phenotyping, and viral persistence markers. Social determinant measures will include education, income, employment, essential-worker status, housing density, food insecurity, neighborhood deprivation index, health insurance, healthcare access, caregiving burden, perceived discrimination, social support, and baseline mental health.\n\nParticipants will complete follow-up assessments at 3, 6, and 12 months using electronic, telephone, and in-person options. Outcomes will include symptom persistence by organ domain, fatigue, dyspnea, cognitive dysfunction, sleep disturbance, pain, dysautonomia symptoms, mental health, return to work or school, healthcare utilization, and quality of life. Validated tools may include PROMIS fatigue and physical function, mMRC dyspnea scale, PHQ-9, GAD-7, post-exertional malaise items, cognitive symptom scales, and a global functional status measure. A subset will undergo objective testing such as spirometry, 6-minute walk testing, orthostatic vitals, neurocognitive testing, and cardiopulmonary or imaging studies when clinically indicated.\n\nThe primary analysis will use multivariable logistic regression and time-to-recovery models to estimate determinants of persistent symptoms at 6 and 12 months, with prespecified domains for clinical severity, biomarkers, and social determinants. Penalized regression or machine learning models will be used for prediction, with internal validation and calibration assessment. Models will adjust for age, sex, race and ethnicity, baseline health, vaccination, reinfection, variant period, and acute disease severity. Missing data will be handled with multiple imputation, and inverse probability weighting will address differential loss to follow-up. Interaction analyses will test whether social disadvantage modifies the association between biological risk markers and persistent symptoms.\n\nThe study will prioritize equity and retention through multilingual consent materials, community-based recruitment, flexible visit formats, transportation support, participant compensation, and linkage to clinical care for concerning findings. Results will produce both etiologic evidence on biological and social pathways of long COVID persistence and a clinically useful risk stratification tool to identify patients who may benefit from early monitoring, rehabilitation, and social support interventions."}
```

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will investigate how urban heat islands affect mental health outcomes and how urban design interventions can reduce psychological harm in heat-vulnerable neighborhoods. topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/energy] Write a free-text research proposal evaluating the potential of agrivoltaics to increase farm income, reduce water use, and expand renewable electricity generat
```
full_prompt: Write a free-text research proposal evaluating the potential of agrivoltaics to increase farm income, reduce water use, and expand renewable electricity generation. Include a methodology for comparing crop yields and energy output across different climates.
completion:
I’ll first gather a concise evidence base on agrivoltaics’ reported impacts on crop yield, water use, and electricity generation, then use it to frame a research proposal.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"agrivoltaics crop yield water use farm income renewable electricity generation climates methodology"}
```

[result]: Search results indicate that agrivoltaics can reduce crop water stress through partial shading, sometimes maintain or increase yields for shade-tolerant crops, and generate additional farm revenue through electricity production or land leases. Impacts vary strongly by crop type, panel height/spacing/tracking design, irrigation regime, and climate zone. Studies commonly compare paired agrivoltaic and open-field plots, monitor microclimate variables, model PV output, and evaluate economic outcomes using net present value or farm income simulations.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"review article on agrivoltaics impacts on crop productivity water use and photovoltaic performance"}
```

[result]: The review emphasizes that agrivoltaic systems create altered microclimates: lower midday solar radiation, reduced evapotranspiration, moderated canopy temperatures, and sometimes higher soil moisture. Benefits are most likely in hot, dry regions and for crops vulnerable to heat or water stress, while yield penalties may occur for light-demanding crops in cloudy or high-latitude climates. Energy output depends on PV density, orientation, tracking, bifacial modules, and cooling effects from vegetation, which can slightly improve panel efficiency.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will evaluate the potential of agrivoltaics to increase farm income, reduce agricultural water use, and expand renewable electricity generation across diverse climate conditions. Agrivoltaics—the co-location of photovoltaic panels and crop production—could allow farmers to diversify revenue while preserving agricultural land, but its performance depends heavily on crop physiology, solar resource, water availability, and system design. The central research question is: under what climatic, agronomic, and market conditions do agrivoltaic systems outperform conventional open-field agriculture and standalone solar development?\n\nThe study will use a multi-site experimental and modeling design across four climate zones: hot-arid, Mediterranean, humid-temperate, and cool/continental. At each site, paired plots will compare conventional crop production with agrivoltaic treatments that vary panel density, height, and orientation. Representative crops will include a leafy green, a fruiting vegetable, a forage crop, and a higher-light-demanding staple crop. Measurements will include crop yield, crop quality, soil moisture, evapotranspiration, irrigation volume, canopy temperature, photosynthetically active radiation, and microclimate conditions. Electricity output will be monitored directly from PV arrays and normalized by installed capacity and land area.\n\nTo compare performance across climates, the project will combine field data with crop-growth and photovoltaic production models. Crop yields will be evaluated using both absolute yield and water productivity, defined as marketable yield per unit of irrigation water or total evapotranspiration. Energy output will be compared using annual kilowatt-hours per hectare, capacity factor, and seasonal generation profiles. Statistical models will estimate how climate variables—solar radiation, temperature extremes, vapor pressure deficit, precipitation, and growing-season length—mediate the relationship between panel design and crop performance. Scenario modeling will then test alternative configurations, including fixed-tilt, single-axis tracking, and bifacial modules.\n\nThe economic analysis will estimate net farm income under three revenue structures: farmer-owned PV systems, land-lease payments from solar developers, and hybrid crop-electricity revenue sharing. Costs will include PV installation, operations and maintenance, altered farm machinery requirements, labor, irrigation infrastructure, and possible yield losses. Benefits will include crop sales, electricity sales, avoided water costs, renewable energy credits, and reduced heat or drought-related crop losses. Metrics will include annual net income, payback period, net present value, and risk-adjusted income stability under drought and energy-price volatility.\n\nThe expected outcome is a climate-specific decision framework identifying where agrivoltaics is most likely to deliver joint benefits for agriculture, water conservation, and clean energy deployment. The project will produce design recommendations for panel spacing, crop selection, and irrigation strategy by climate zone, as well as policy guidance on incentives that reward both renewable electricity generation and agricultural resilience. By directly comparing crop yields, water use, and PV output across climates, the research will help determine whether agrivoltaics can become a scalable land-use strategy for decarbonization and climate-adapted farming."}
```

```action

```

[result]: Recent research highlights the value of Indigenous cultural burning, community-led fire stewardship, and Indigenous ecological knowledge for reducing fuel loads and restoring fire-adapted ecosystems. Remote sensing studies use satellite-derived vegetation indices, soil moisture, drought stress, land surface temperature, and fuel moisture estimates to predict wildfire risk. Emerging interdisciplinary work emphasizes co-production of climate knowledge, data sovereignty, and ethical integration of Indigenous knowledge with geospatial modeling.

I’ll read one representative source on co-production and Indigenous fire stewardship to refine the governance and methods of the proposal.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"Representative review on Indigenous fire stewardship, remote sensing, and co-produced wildfire management"}
```

[result]: The review argues that Indigenous knowledge should not be treated as a data layer to be extracted, but as part of a living governance system involving place-based observation, cultural protocols, seasonal indicators, and stewardship responsibilities. It recommends collaborative model design, Indigenous data sovereignty agreements, community validation of satellite outputs, and decision-support tools that serve local land-management priorities rather than only centralized fire suppression goals.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project proposes a co-produced wildfire prediction and land-management framework that combines Indigenous ecological knowledge with satellite-based climate and vegetation modeling. Rather than treating Indigenous knowledge as an auxiliary dataset, the project would partner with Indigenous fire practitioners, land stewards, and governance bodies to identify locally meaningful indicators of fire risk, such as plant phenology, animal behavior, wind patterns, fuel condition, seasonal moisture cues, and culturally significant landscape histories. These observations would be integrated with satellite-derived measures including vegetation greenness, fuel moisture, land surface temperature, drought indices, soil moisture, burn scars, and projected climate anomalies.\n\nMethodologically, the project would develop a layered prediction model in which satellite data provide broad spatial and temporal coverage, while Indigenous ecological knowledge guides interpretation, calibration, and management relevance at the landscape scale. For example, remote sensing may detect dry vegetation, but Indigenous fire practitioners may distinguish between areas needing cultural burning, areas where fire would damage important species, and areas where historical fire exclusion has created abnormal fuel buildup. Machine-learning or Bayesian models could be designed to incorporate both quantitative satellite variables and community-defined risk categories, with uncertainty estimates and local validation built into the workflow.\n\nA central component would be ethical governance. Participating Indigenous communities would determine what knowledge can be shared, how it is represented, who controls access, and how outputs are used. The project would follow Indigenous data sovereignty principles and create decision-support tools that serve community priorities, such as planning cultural burns, protecting sacred sites, restoring fire-adapted habitats, and coordinating with regional fire agencies. Sensitive cultural knowledge would not need to be digitized or publicly mapped; instead, it could inform model structure, interpretation, and management decisions through controlled community-led processes.\n\nThe expected outcome is a more accurate and socially responsible wildfire prediction system that moves beyond emergency suppression toward proactive land stewardship. By combining satellite climate modeling with Indigenous fire knowledge, the project could improve early warning, identify areas where prescribed or cultural burning would reduce risk, and support restoration of resilient fire-adapted ecosystems. More broadly, it would demonstrate an interdisciplinary model for climate adaptation that respects Indigenous authority while strengthening wildfire science and land-management practice."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/interdisciplinary] Explore how generative AI tools are reshaping authorship, creativity, and labor in the publishing industry, drawing from computer science, sociology, and litera
full_prompt: Explore how generative AI tools are reshaping authorship, creativity, and labor in the publishing industry, drawing from computer science, sociology, and literary studies.
completion:
I’ll first scan for recent interdisciplinary work connecting generative AI, publishing labor, authorship norms, and creative practice.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"generative AI authorship creativity labor publishing industry computer science sociology literary studies 2023 2024"}
```

[result]: Simulated results indicate several relevant clusters:  
1. Computer science work on large language models, human-AI co-writing, provenance, watermarking, and evaluation of creative text generation.  
2. Sociology and media-industry studies on automation, platformization, deskilling/reskilling, freelance precarity, and copyright conflicts in cultural labor.  
3. Literary studies debates on authorship, originality, intertextuality, genre fiction, fan fiction, and the changing status of the “author” when writing becomes collaborative with machines.  
4. Publishing-industry reports discussing AI-assisted editing, marketing copy, audiobook narration, translation, rights management, and concerns from authors’ guilds and editorial workers.

To ground the proposal, I’ll inspect one representative source cluster on cultural labor and generative AI in publishing.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"recent interdisciplinary articles and industry reports on generative AI in publishing labor, authorship, and creative writing"}
```

[result]: Simulated reading summary: The literature suggests that generative AI is not simply replacing authors but redistributing creative agency across authors, editors, platforms, datasets, and model providers. In publishing, AI tools are being adopted unevenly: large firms experiment with workflow automation, while freelancers and genre writers often encounter AI through productivity pressure and market competition. Key unresolved issues include attribution, consent for training data, copyright ownership, editorial deskilling, the visibility of human labor, and whether AI-generated or AI-assisted texts alter literary value.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project investigates how generative AI tools are reshaping authorship, creativity, and labor in the publishing industry by combining methods from computer science, sociology, and literary studies. Its central research question is: when AI systems participate in writing, editing, marketing, translation, and narration, how are creative agency and professional responsibility redistributed among human authors, publishing workers, platforms, and machine-learning systems? Rather than treating AI as either a neutral tool or an autonomous creator, the project will analyze publishing as a sociotechnical system in which literary production is increasingly mediated by proprietary models, datasets, interfaces, and workplace policies.\n\nThe project will use a mixed-methods design. From computer science, it will examine the affordances and limits of generative AI systems used for drafting, copyediting, summarization, cover copy, translation, and metadata generation, with attention to provenance, bias, style imitation, and detectability. From sociology, it will conduct interviews with authors, editors, literary agents, translators, audiobook workers, and publishing managers to understand how AI adoption changes workflows, job security, professional identity, and bargaining power. From literary studies, it will analyze AI-assisted texts and public controversies around them in relation to older theories of authorship, originality, intertextuality, genre convention, and the death or persistence of the author.\n\nA key contribution will be to distinguish several emerging models of AI-mediated authorship: AI as assistant, AI as ghostwriter, AI as editor, AI as market analyst, and AI as synthetic competitor. Each model implies different ethical and legal questions. For example, AI as assistant may intensify existing creative labor while leaving the author’s name intact; AI as ghostwriter challenges attribution and transparency; AI as market analyst may push literary production toward data-driven sameness; and AI as synthetic competitor may affect wages and visibility for freelance writers, translators, and narrators. The project will therefore connect textual analysis with labor analysis, showing that debates over creativity cannot be separated from ownership, compensation, and control over infrastructure.\n\nThe expected outcome is an interdisciplinary account of generative AI in publishing that moves beyond simple claims of replacement or innovation. The project will produce a typology of AI-assisted publishing labor, a set of case studies of changing authorship practices, and policy recommendations for transparency, credit, consent, and worker protection. Ultimately, it argues that generative AI is not ending authorship but making authorship more distributed, contested, and dependent on hidden technical and human infrastructures. Understanding this transformation requires reading AI-generated texts not only as literary artifacts, but also as evidence of changing labor relations in contemporary cultural production."}
```

Precision-agriculture soil monitoring commonly targets moisture, temperature, pH, electrical conductivity, nitrate/phosphate/potassium availability, microbial activity, and organic matter proxies. Key challenges include sensor calibration in heterogeneous soils, degradation-rate control, wireless data transmission, ecological safety of degradation byproducts, and integration of sensor streams with machine-learning models for farm decision support.

To refine the ecological and technical framing, I’ll read a representative source on biodegradable environmental sensors and soil-health monitoring gaps.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"review biodegradable transient electronics environmental sensors soil monitoring ecological impact"}
```

[result]: Review findings suggest that biodegradable soil sensors can reduce electronic waste and enable high-density spatial monitoring, but deployment must account for soil texture, moisture, pH, microbial communities, and seasonal dynamics that affect both sensor performance and material degradation. There is growing interest in coupling biodegradable substrates with low-power wireless communication, energy harvesting, and analytics platforms, but few studies validate sensor accuracy, biodegradation, and ecological effects together under real farm conditions.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project proposes to develop and validate biodegradable sensor networks for monitoring soil health in precision agriculture by integrating materials science, ecology, and data analytics. The central hypothesis is that transient, soil-safe sensors made from biodegradable polymers, biopolymers, and benign conductive elements can provide sufficiently accurate, spatially dense soil-health data while minimizing electronic waste and ecological disturbance. The study will focus on key soil indicators—moisture, temperature, pH, electrical conductivity, and nutrient proxies such as nitrate availability—because these variables directly influence crop productivity, microbial function, and fertilizer-management decisions.\n\nThe materials-science component will design sensor platforms using biodegradable substrates such as cellulose, chitosan, silk fibroin, PLA, or PHA, paired with environmentally benign conductive materials including carbon-based inks, magnesium, zinc, or molybdenum traces. Laboratory experiments will evaluate sensitivity, drift, mechanical stability, and degradation rates across contrasting soil textures, moisture levels, pH ranges, and microbial activity profiles. A major design goal will be tunable lifetime: sensors should remain functional through a growing season and then degrade into non-toxic byproducts without requiring retrieval.\n\nThe ecological component will test whether sensor deployment and degradation alter soil processes. Greenhouse and field trials will compare sensor-treated and untreated plots across crop systems, measuring microbial respiration, enzyme activity, earthworm response, nutrient cycling, organic matter dynamics, and plant performance. This will allow the project to assess not only whether the sensors measure soil health, but also whether they preserve it. Particular attention will be given to how local soil ecology influences material breakdown and sensor reliability, creating feedback between ecological context and sensor design.\n\nThe data-analytics component will build a pipeline that converts distributed sensor measurements into actionable soil-health maps and management recommendations. Sensor data will be integrated with weather records, satellite or drone imagery, soil survey data, and crop-yield outcomes. Machine-learning models will be trained to detect nutrient stress, irrigation needs, and spatial variability in soil function, while uncertainty estimates will account for sensor degradation, calibration drift, and environmental heterogeneity. The final output will be a decision-support prototype that helps farmers optimize irrigation and fertilizer application while reducing waste and environmental impact.\n\nThe expected outcome is a validated framework for biodegradable soil-sensor networks that combines functional materials, ecological safety testing, and predictive analytics. By addressing sensor performance, biodegradation behavior, and farm-scale data interpretation together, the project will advance sustainable precision agriculture and offer an alternative to persistent electronic monitoring devices. The broader impact is a pathway toward high-resolution soil intelligence that supports productivity, reduces agrochemical losses, and aligns digital agriculture with circular-material principles."}
```


[result]: Recent literature suggests remote work can reduce commuting emissions but may increase residential energy use and non-work travel. Studies on family dynamics report mixed effects: greater schedule flexibility and parental involvement, but also blurred work-family boundaries and gendered burdens. Economic geography research indicates remote work may redistribute income toward lower-cost regions, but can also intensify housing pressures and widen gaps between “remote-work-capable” and place-bound workers.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This interdisciplinary research project will assess how the expansion of remote work affects carbon emissions, family dynamics, and regional economic inequality. The central premise is that remote work is not a single environmental or social intervention, but a reorganization of daily life with uneven consequences across households, occupations, and places. The study will ask: under what conditions does remote work reduce net emissions; how does it reshape caregiving, household labor, and work-family boundaries; and does it narrow or widen economic inequality between regions and worker groups?\n\nThe project will combine environmental analysis, household sociology, and regional economics. First, it will estimate emissions changes by comparing reduced commuting with increased home energy use, altered travel patterns, and potential residential relocation. Second, it will examine family dynamics through surveys and interviews focused on time use, caregiving responsibilities, gender division of labor, stress, and perceived work-life balance. Third, it will analyze regional inequality using labor-market, housing, wage, and migration data to identify whether remote work disperses opportunity beyond major metropolitan centers or instead benefits already-advantaged workers and regions.\n\nMethodologically, the study will use a mixed-methods design. Quantitative analysis will link worker-level remote work frequency with emissions estimates, household survey responses, and regional indicators such as median wages, housing costs, employment growth, and tax-base shifts. Qualitative interviews with remote workers, hybrid workers, non-remote workers, caregivers, employers, and local policymakers will provide insight into mechanisms that aggregate data may miss, including household negotiation, isolation, career advancement concerns, and community-level change.\n\nThe expected contribution is a policy-relevant framework for evaluating remote work as a climate, family, and regional development issue simultaneously. Rather than asking whether remote work is simply “good” or “bad,” the project will identify the conditions under which it produces net social benefit. Findings could inform employer hybrid-work policies, urban and regional planning, energy-efficiency programs for homes, childcare and caregiving supports, and strategies to ensure that remote work does not deepen inequalities between high-income knowledge workers and workers whose jobs remain geographically fixed."}
```

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This interdisciplinary research project will examine whether personalized music therapy supported by wearable biosensors can reduce stress among hospitalized adult patients. The central research question is: can real-time physiological stress indicators from wearable biosensors help tailor music therapy sessions and improve stress reduction compared with standard music therapy or usual care?\n\nThe project will use a mixed-methods pilot randomized controlled design with three groups: usual care, scheduled music therapy, and biosensor-informed personalized music therapy. In the biosensor-informed group, wearable devices will continuously collect heart rate variability, heart rate, electrodermal activity, sleep, and movement data to identify periods of heightened stress and guide timing or adaptation of sessions.\n\nOutcomes will include patient-reported stress, anxiety, pain, sleep quality, and satisfaction, alongside physiological measures such as heart rate variability, resting heart rate, and electrodermal activity. The study will also collect clinical indicators such as sedative or analgesic medication use, length of stay, and nursing-reported agitation or distress where relevant. Qualitative interviews with patients, music therapists, nurses, and physicians will explore acceptability, perceived benefits, privacy concerns, workflow burden, and whether biosensor feedback meaningfully improves therapeutic personalization.\n\nThe project’s contribution is both clinical and methodological. Clinically, it will evaluate a low-risk, nonpharmacological intervention for stress reduction in hospital settings. Methodologically, it will test how wearable biosensors can be integrated with human-centered therapeutic care without reducing music therapy to a purely algorithmic intervention. Findings will inform a larger multisite trial and produce practical guidelines for using biosensor data ethically and effectively in supportive hospital care."}
```

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will investigate whether food insecurity is associated with lower gut microbiome diversity and poorer academic performance among adolescents, and whether microbiome diversity helps explain part of this relationship. The central hypothesis is that food insecurity reduces dietary quality and dietary diversity, increases stress, and disrupts routines, which together may be associated with lower gut microbial diversity and weaker academic outcomes such as grades, attendance, and standardized test performance.\n\nThe study will use a longitudinal mixed-methods design with adolescents ages 12–17 recruited through middle and high schools in socioeconomically diverse communities. Nutrition science methods will include validated household food insecurity screening, 24-hour dietary recalls, food frequency measures, and assessment of dietary diversity, fiber intake, ultra-processed food consumption, and school meal participation. Microbiology methods will include stool sample collection at two or three time points across the school year, followed by 16S rRNA gene sequencing or shotgun metagenomic profiling to estimate alpha diversity, beta diversity, and the relative abundance of taxa or functional pathways associated with diet and inflammation.\n\nEducation research methods will link biological and nutrition data to academic indicators, including GPA, attendance, disciplinary records where appropriate, and standardized assessment scores, with consent and privacy protections. Surveys will also measure sleep, perceived stress, depressive symptoms, physical activity, antibiotic use, gastrointestinal symptoms, and household socioeconomic conditions. Multilevel regression and mediation models will test whether food insecurity predicts microbiome diversity and academic outcomes, while accounting for student-, household-, school-, and neighborhood-level confounders. Moderation analyses will examine whether school meal participation or dietary quality buffers associations between food insecurity and microbiome or academic outcomes.\n\nA qualitative component will involve interviews or focus groups with adolescents and caregivers to understand lived experiences of food access, school meals, stress, eating patterns, and barriers to healthy routines. These data will help interpret biological and educational findings and avoid overly deterministic explanations of academic performance. The expected contribution is an integrated model showing how nutritional deprivation, biological embodiment, and educational inequality may interact during adolescence, while identifying modifiable intervention points such as improving school meal quality, expanding food assistance access, and supporting student health as part of academic achievement strategies."}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/interdisciplinary] Propose a free-text interdisciplinary study on the ethical, legal, and psychological implications of using brain-computer interfaces in education.
full_prompt: Propose a free-text interdisciplinary study on the ethical, legal, and psychological implications of using brain-computer interfaces in education.
completion:
I will first scan recent interdisciplinary discussions to anchor the proposal in current concerns around educational neurotechnology, privacy, consent, and student wellbeing.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"brain-computer interfaces education ethics law psychology student privacy consent cognitive monitoring"}
```

[result]: Recent scholarship and policy discussions emphasize that brain-computer interfaces and related neurotechnologies in education raise concerns about neural data privacy, informed consent, cognitive liberty, disability access, algorithmic bias, surveillance, psychological pressure, and unclear legal protections for students. Key themes include whether brain data should receive special legal status, how minors can meaningfully consent, risks of normalizing attention monitoring, and the potential benefits of assistive BCIs for students with disabilities.

To sharpen the proposal, I will read a representative source focused on neural data governance and educational implications.

```action
{"tool":"read","source":"overview article on neurotechnology governance, neural data privacy, and educational applications"}
```

[result]: The source argues that neural data can reveal sensitive information about attention, emotion, fatigue, and possibly health status. It recommends sector-specific safeguards for schools, including strict limits on data collection, prohibition of punitive uses, transparent algorithms, independent oversight, and special protections for children and disabled students. It also notes a tension between beneficial personalization and coercive surveillance.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This interdisciplinary study would examine the ethical, legal, and psychological implications of using brain-computer interfaces in education, focusing on how BCIs might transform learning environments, student autonomy, and institutional responsibility. The project would bring together education studies, bioethics, law, psychology, neuroscience, disability studies, and human-computer interaction to ask a central question: under what conditions, if any, can BCIs be used in schools or universities without undermining students’ rights, wellbeing, and equal participation?\n\nThe study would compare three likely educational uses of BCIs: assistive communication for students with disabilities, adaptive learning systems that respond to cognitive load or attention, and classroom monitoring technologies that track engagement or fatigue. Ethically, it would analyze issues of cognitive liberty, mental privacy, informed consent, and fairness. Particular attention would be paid to the vulnerability of children and students who may feel unable to refuse BCI use when it is promoted by teachers, parents, universities, or technology vendors. The study would also examine whether BCIs could reduce barriers for some learners while creating new forms of exclusion for students whose neural patterns do not fit algorithmic norms.\n\nLegally, the project would investigate whether existing privacy, disability, education, and data protection laws are sufficient to govern neural data in educational settings. It would ask whether brain-derived data should be treated as ordinary biometric data or as a special category requiring stronger protection. The legal component would examine questions of ownership, consent, data retention, third-party access, discrimination, and liability if BCI-generated interpretations are inaccurate or harmful. The study would also consider whether schools should be prohibited from using BCI data for discipline, grading, admissions, or behavioral prediction.\n\nPsychologically, the study would explore how BCI use affects students’ self-concept, motivation, anxiety, attention, and trust in educators. A mixed-methods design would combine policy analysis, interviews with students, parents, teachers, disability advocates, and technology developers, and experimental or survey-based research using hypothetical BCI classroom scenarios. This would allow the project to assess not only what people think about educational BCIs, but also how different governance models—such as opt-in consent, anonymization, or bans on performance monitoring—shape perceived safety and willingness to participate.\n\nThe expected outcome would be a practical ethical and legal framework for educational BCI use. The study would propose guidelines such as prioritizing assistive and student-controlled applications, banning coercive attention surveillance, requiring independent audits of algorithms, limiting neural data collection to clearly justified purposes, and creating age-appropriate consent procedures. By integrating legal analysis with psychological evidence and ethical theory, the project would help educators and policymakers distinguish beneficial neurotechnology from intrusive cognitive surveillance."}
```

topic: [v15/zh/gene_genome/computer_science] 设想一种用于检测深度伪造内容的跨域泛化算法，描述其技术路线、潜在挑战以及如何证明其优于现有方法。
full_prompt: 设想一种用于检测深度伪造内容的跨域泛化算法，描述其技术路线、潜在挑战以及如何证明其优于现有方法。
completion:
我先检索跨域深度伪造检测的近期方法，重点关注域泛化、自监督伪迹学习与频域/时域线索融合。随后基于已有不足提出一个可验证的算法假设。

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"cross-domain generalization deepfake detection domain generalization frequency artifact self-supervised benchmark"}
```

[result]: 近期方法多依赖伪造数据集特定伪迹，如压缩痕迹、生成器指纹或人脸局部纹理；跨数据集测试时性能显著下降。常见改进包括频域特征、元学习域泛化、数据增强、自监督预训练和多模态时序一致性，但仍难以区分“伪造相关因果线索”和“数据集偏置线索”。

我进一步阅读一篇代表性方向，确认现有方法在解释性与跨生成器鲁棒性上仍有空间。

```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"Representative survey and benchmark papers on generalized deepfake detection"}
```

[result]: 综述指出，FaceForensics++、Celeb-DF、DFDC、WildDeepfake 等数据分布差异很大；模型在源域高准确率不代表目标域可靠。较有前景的路线是学习生成过程不变的伪迹表示，并通过留一域验证、未知生成器测试、真实压缩扰动和跨身份/跨场景划分证明泛化能力。

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"提出一种因果不变伪迹解耦的跨域深度伪造检测算法：模型由三部分组成。第一，使用视觉主干提取空间纹理、频域谱残差和短时运动一致性特征；第二，引入域对抗学习与因果瓶颈模块，将特征分解为伪造因果特征 z_c 和域/数据集偏置特征 z_d，并通过互信息最小化抑制 z_c 对数据集、压缩率、身份、分辨率的依赖；第三，使用伪造机制一致性约束，让来自不同生成器但同为伪造的视频在 z_c 空间中靠近，而真实样本远离，同时加入跨压缩、跨分辨率、颜色扰动和重编码增强以逼近真实部署分布。","niche_genome":"该算法面向跨数据集、跨生成器、跨压缩条件下的深度伪造检测，尤其适用于训练集只覆盖少数伪造算法，而测试时面对未知生成模型、社交媒体压缩和复杂拍摄条件的场景。","observation_genome":"现有检测器往往在同数据集上表现很好，但在未知数据集上显著退化，说明它们学习到了数据集特定伪迹而非伪造的稳定因果线索。频域方法、时序方法和域泛化方法各有优势，但通常没有显式区分伪造因果特征与采集/压缩/身份等混杂因素。","limitation_genome":"潜在挑战包括：伪造因果线索本身可能随生成技术演进而变化；域标签和生成器标签可能不完整；过强的域不变约束可能删除有用判别信息；视频级时序建模计算成本较高；真实部署中还会遇到低清、遮挡、二次拍摄和对抗性后处理。","delta_genome":"相较现有方法，关键差异是把跨域泛化问题表述为伪造因果特征与域偏置特征的可学习解耦，而不仅是简单数据增强或域对抗。技术上结合频域谱残差、局部生理/运动一致性、互信息约束、域不变对比学习和留一生成器元训练，以获得更稳定的未知域判别边界。","claim_genome":"可通过严格实验验证其优越性：在 FaceForensics++、Celeb-DF、DFDC、WildDeepfake、DeeperForensics 等数据集上做 leave-one-dataset-out 和 leave-one-manipulation-out 测试；与 Xception、EfficientNet、F3-Net、SPSL、RECCE、域泛化深度伪造检测器等基线比较 AUC、EER、F1 和校准误差；进行压缩率、分辨率、未知生成器和社交媒体重编码鲁棒性测试；用消融实验验证频域、时序、互信息解耦、域对抗和对比损失各自贡献。若该方法在源域相近性能下显著提升目标未知域 AUC/EER，并在解释性可视化中更关注面部边界、眨眼/口型不一致和频谱异常等稳定伪迹，而非背景或压缩块效应，则可证明其优于现有方法。"}}
```

topic: [v15/zh/gene_genome/computer_science] 围绕联邦学习中的隐私保护与通信效率问题，提出一个可验证的研究设想，并说明可能的实验数据集和评价指标。
full_prompt: 围绕联邦学习中的隐私保护与通信效率问题，提出一个可验证的研究设想，并说明可能的实验数据集和评价指标。
completion:
为定位研究空白，先检索联邦学习中差分隐私与通信压缩的交叉工作，关注“隐私噪声是否会抵消压缩收益”以及“压缩是否改变隐私会计”这两个问题。  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"federated learning differential privacy communication compression quantization sparsification secure aggregation"}
```
[result]: 相关工作显示：DP-FedAvg 通常通过裁剪与高斯噪声实现客户端级隐私，但会降低精度；FedPAQ、Top-k sparsification、QSGD 等方法可降低通信量，但多数未系统分析与客户端级 DP、secure aggregation、非 IID 数据共同作用下的效用损失。一个可研究空白是：联合设计“隐私噪声注入位置”和“压缩/量化策略”，而非简单串联 DP 与压缩。

进一步阅读一类代表性通信压缩方法，确认误差反馈与稀疏化在非 IID 场景下的优势和潜在隐私风险。  
```action
{"tool":"read","paper":"Federated Learning with Communication-Efficient Gradient Sparsification and Error Feedback"}
```
[result]: 误差反馈可缓解 Top-k 稀疏化带来的优化偏差，但残差缓冲区会长期保存未上传更新，若直接与 DP 组合，可能形成额外隐私暴露面；因此需要对残差进行裁剪、噪声化或安全聚合约束。评价通常包括测试准确率、通信字节数、收敛轮数和客户端异质性下的鲁棒性。

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"提出一种 Privacy-Aware Error-Feedback Quantized FL 方法：客户端先对本地模型更新进行范数裁剪，然后基于更新方差自适应选择 Top-k 稀疏坐标；对保留坐标加入满足客户端级差分隐私的高斯噪声，并进行低比特量化；未上传残差进入误差反馈缓冲区，但缓冲区本身周期性裁剪并噪声化，最后通过 secure aggregation 聚合。","niche_genome":"面向跨设备联邦学习中同时存在隐私预算受限、上行带宽受限和客户端数据非 IID 的场景，例如移动键盘预测、手写字符识别和端侧图像分类。","observation_genome":"现有方法常将差分隐私与通信压缩简单串联：先加噪再压缩可能放大量化误差，先压缩再加噪可能降低有效信号；同时，误差反馈缓冲区虽提升收敛效率，却可能累积敏感更新信息。","limitation_genome":"该设想依赖较准确的隐私会计和客户端采样假设；在极端非 IID 或极低隐私预算下，噪声与稀疏化可能共同导致模型精度显著下降；secure aggregation 的额外协议开销也需要单独统计。","delta_genome":"相较于 DP-FedAvg、FedPAQ、Top-k sparsification+DP 等基线，该方法的增量在于联合优化裁剪、噪声注入、稀疏化、量化和误差反馈残差管理，而不是把隐私保护和通信压缩作为两个独立模块。","claim_genome":"可验证假设：在相同客户端级隐私预算例如 epsilon=2,4,8 和 delta=1e-5 下，该方法相较 DP-FedAvg 可减少至少 50% 上行通信量，并在 FEMNIST、Shakespeare、CIFAR-10/100 联邦划分数据集上保持不超过 1-3 个百分点的测试精度损失；评价指标包括测试准确率/困惑度、总上传字节数、达到目标精度所需通信轮数、隐私预算 epsilon、客户端参与率、非 IID 程度下的性能下降，以及 secure aggregation 后的端到端训练时间。"}}
```

topic: [v15/zh/gene_genome/computer_science] 请提出一项关于图神经网络在程序分析中的应用研究，说明如何利用代码结构信息提升漏洞检测或错误定位性能。
full_prompt: 请提出一项关于图神经网络在程序分析中的应用研究，说明如何利用代码结构信息提升漏洞检测或错误定位性能。
completion:
先检索图神经网络用于程序分析/漏洞检测的代表性脉络，确认现有方法主要利用 AST、CFG、DFG/PDG 等结构信息，但常面临跨结构融合不足与定位粒度不细的问题。

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"graph neural networks program analysis vulnerability detection AST CFG DFG PDG bug localization"}
```

[result]: 代表性研究显示，GNN 已被用于代码表征、漏洞检测、缺陷预测和错误定位；常见图结构包括抽象语法树 AST、控制流图 CFG、数据流图 DFG、程序依赖图 PDG。现有方法通常将代码转为单一图或简单拼接多种图，容易忽略“语法-控制-数据依赖”之间的异构关系，且多数输出为函数级/文件级分类，难以解释具体漏洞位置。

基于上述缺口，进一步聚焦一个可研究的问题：构建异构程序图并进行边类型感知消息传递，将漏洞检测与语句级定位联合建模。

```action
{"tool":"read","paper_or_topic":"heterogeneous graph neural networks for code vulnerability detection and localization"}
```

[result]: 异构 GNN 可通过不同节点/边类型建模复杂关系；在代码场景中，可将 token、AST 节点、语句、变量、函数调用作为节点，将语法父子边、控制依赖边、数据依赖边、调用边、变量定义-使用边作为边。联合学习分类与定位任务有助于提升模型可解释性，但需要处理图规模大、跨项目泛化弱和标注稀缺等问题。

```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"提出一种面向漏洞检测与错误定位的异构程序图神经网络框架。该方法将源代码解析为融合 AST、CFG、DFG/PDG 与调用关系的异构程序图，节点包括 token、语句、变量、函数和控制块，边包括语法边、控制流边、数据依赖边、定义-使用边和函数调用边。模型采用边类型感知的消息传递机制，并引入跨结构注意力模块，自适应学习语法结构、控制依赖和数据依赖对不同漏洞类型的贡献。","niche_genome":"研究聚焦于 C/C++ 或 Java 程序中的安全漏洞检测和语句级错误定位，尤其适用于空指针、缓冲区溢出、资源泄漏、未检查返回值等依赖控制流和数据流上下文的漏洞。相比仅使用 token 序列或单一 AST/CFG 的方法，该研究强调多源代码结构信息的统一建模。","observation_genome":"现有深度学习漏洞检测方法常能捕获局部词法模式，但对长距离数据依赖、跨分支控制条件和函数间调用关系建模不足；而传统静态分析虽可利用结构信息，但规则依赖强、误报较高。GNN 能在程序图上传播语义信息，因此有潜力同时提升漏洞检测准确率和定位可解释性。","limitation_genome":"潜在限制包括：构建完整程序依赖图成本较高；大型项目中的图规模可能导致训练和推理开销增加；高质量语句级漏洞标签稀缺；模型可能学习到项目特定编码风格而非真正漏洞语义，导致跨项目泛化下降。","delta_genome":"相较已有方法，本研究的增量在于：1）构建统一的异构程序图，而不是单独使用 AST、CFG 或 DFG；2）设计边类型感知与跨结构注意力结合的 GNN，使模型区分不同结构关系的重要性；3）采用漏洞检测和语句级定位的多任务学习，让分类信号与定位信号相互增强；4）通过边/节点注意力或子图抽取提供可解释的漏洞证据。","claim_genome":"假设该异构结构感知 GNN 能显著提升漏洞检测 F1、AUC 以及语句级 Top-k 定位准确率。核心主张是：将代码的语法结构、控制流和数据依赖统一为异构图，并进行关系感知消息传递，可以比纯序列模型或单图 GNN 更有效地捕获漏洞产生的程序语义条件，从而提升漏洞检测性能和错误定位质量。"}} 
```

[result]: 已提出研究方案：基于异构程序图神经网络融合 AST、CFG、DFG/PDG 和调用关系，用于提升代码漏洞检测与语句级错误定位性能。

topic: [v15/zh/gene_genome/computer_science] 请撰写一份关于面向大规模语言模型的高效推理加速方法的自由文本研究提案，重点讨论模型压缩、推理调度与硬件协同优化。
full_prompt: 请撰写一份关于面向大规模语言模型的高效推理加速方法的自由文本研究提案，重点讨论模型压缩、推理调度与硬件协同优化。
completion:
我先检索近期关于 LLM 推理加速的代表性方向，以便将提案聚焦在模型压缩、调度系统与硬件协同三者的交叉空白上。  
```action
{"tool":"search","query":"efficient LLM inference acceleration model compression scheduling hardware co-design KV cache speculative decoding quantization"}
```
[result]: 近期工作集中在：1) 低比特量化、稀疏化、蒸馏、MoE/早退等模型压缩；2) continuous batching、PagedAttention、KV cache 管理、speculative decoding、prefill/decode 分离等推理调度；3) GPU/TPU/NPU 上的算子融合、内存层级优化、低精度张量核利用和近存计算。主要不足是三类方法常被单独优化，缺少面向端到端服务目标的联合设计框架。

基于检索结果，我提出一个“压缩—调度—硬件”闭环协同的研究方案，核心是让模型结构、运行时策略和硬件执行特性共同参与优化，而不是逐层堆叠独立技巧。  
```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"本研究拟提出一种面向大规模语言模型的高效推理加速框架，将模型压缩、推理调度与硬件协同优化统一建模。机制上，首先构建硬件感知的模型压缩模块，对 Transformer 层中的权重、激活与 KV cache 进行混合精度量化、结构化稀疏化和轻量蒸馏；其次设计运行时调度器，根据请求长度、batch 动态性、prefill/decode 阶段特征、KV cache 占用和硬件吞吐状态，进行 continuous batching、请求重排、内存分页和投机解码策略选择；最后引入硬件协同优化层，将压缩后的算子形态映射到 GPU/NPU 张量核、片上 SRAM、HBM 带宽和通信拓扑上，通过算子融合、低比特 kernel、KV cache 分块布局和多设备流水并行降低端到端延迟。","niche_genome":"该提案面向云端与边缘端 LLM 推理服务中的高并发、长上下文、多租户和成本敏感场景。不同于单纯追求模型参数减少或单个 kernel 加速，本研究关注服务级指标，包括 token latency、吞吐、显存占用、能耗、请求尾延迟和精度退化之间的联合权衡，尤其适合部署 7B 到 70B 级别模型以及多模型共享硬件的推理平台。","observation_genome":"现有 LLM 推理瓶颈具有明显阶段性：prefill 阶段主要受矩阵乘法吞吐限制，decode 阶段则常受 KV cache 读写和内存带宽限制。模型压缩可以降低计算和存储需求，但若压缩粒度与硬件 kernel 不匹配，可能引入额外反量化或不规则访存开销；推理调度可以提升吞吐，但若忽视模型精度配置和显存碎片，也会导致尾延迟升高；硬件优化可以提高单算子性能，但若不考虑请求动态性，则难以带来端到端收益。因此，三者之间存在强耦合关系。","limitation_genome":"本研究的主要挑战在于：第一，压缩策略、调度策略和硬件参数的联合搜索空间极大，直接优化成本高；第二，不同模型架构、上下文长度和硬件平台之间存在迁移难题；第三，低比特量化、稀疏化和投机解码可能引入精度波动，需要建立可控的质量保证机制；第四，多用户服务场景下还需处理公平性、隔离性和 SLA 约束，避免仅优化平均吞吐而牺牲尾部请求体验。","delta_genome":"相较于已有方法，本提案的增量在于提出一个跨层联合优化框架：1) 在模型压缩阶段引入硬件代价模型，使量化位宽、稀疏模式和 KV cache 压缩策略与目标设备的 kernel 支持和内存层级匹配；2) 在推理调度阶段引入模型状态感知，使调度器能根据不同层、不同 token 阶段和不同压缩配置动态选择 batch、投机步长和 cache 布局；3) 在硬件执行阶段引入调度反馈，使 kernel fusion、低比特 GEMM、attention kernel 和显存分页机制根据在线负载自适应调整。","claim_genome":"预期成果是一套面向 LLM 推理的端到端加速系统，可在保持模型质量基本不变的前提下显著降低推理成本。研究假设是：通过压缩、调度与硬件协同设计，相比仅使用量化、仅使用 continuous batching 或仅优化 kernel 的单点方法，可同时实现更高 token 吞吐、更低 p95/p99 延迟、更小显存占用和更优能效。实验将覆盖不同规模模型、不同上下文长度和不同硬件平台，并以精度保持率、tokens/s、TTFT、TPOT、显存峰值、能耗和服务成本作为核心评估指标。"}}
```

基于以上观察，提出一个将“静态可达性证据”与“依赖生态图机器学习”结合的研究方案，用于预测和解释依赖漏洞传播风险。

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/economics_finance] Write a free-text research proposal examining how central bank digital currencies could affect commercial bank deposit stability and monetary policy transmissio
full_prompt: Write a free-text research proposal examining how central bank digital currencies could affect commercial bank deposit stability and monetary policy transmission. Include a potential identification strategy and key data sources.
completion:
I’ll first scan for recent policy and empirical references on CBDCs, bank deposits, and monetary transmission to ground the proposal in current economics/finance debates.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"central bank digital currency commercial bank deposits monetary policy transmission empirical identification deposit stability"}
```

[result]: Recent relevant sources include BIS and IMF work on CBDC design and bank intermediation, central bank reports on CBDC pilots, and empirical studies using payment-system shocks, deposit-rate pass-through, and digital money adoption to assess effects on bank funding and monetary transmission.

To refine the empirical angle, I’ll read a representative policy/economic synthesis on CBDCs and bank intermediation.

```action
{"tool":"read","query":"BIS CBDC bank deposits intermediation monetary policy transmission design safeguards"}
```

[result]: The source emphasizes that CBDCs could increase competition for bank deposits, especially in stress periods, but effects depend strongly on design features such as remuneration, holding limits, tiered interest rates, wallet access, and convertibility. It also notes that CBDCs may alter monetary transmission by changing deposit-rate competition, reserve demand, and the speed with which policy-rate changes pass through to households and firms.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project will examine how retail central bank digital currencies (CBDCs) affect commercial bank deposit stability and the transmission of monetary policy. The central hypothesis is that CBDCs introduce a new safe, liquid outside option for households and firms, increasing the interest elasticity and stress sensitivity of bank deposits. However, the magnitude of this effect should depend on CBDC design: non-interest-bearing CBDCs with holding caps may have limited effects, while remunerated or uncapped CBDCs could materially weaken banks’ deposit franchises and accelerate deposit outflows during periods of financial uncertainty.\n\nThe project will develop and test two linked mechanisms. First, CBDCs may reduce deposit stability by making it easier for depositors to move funds from commercial banks into central bank money, particularly when bank risk rises or deposit rates lag policy rates. Second, CBDCs may change monetary policy transmission by increasing competitive pressure on banks to adjust deposit rates after policy-rate changes. This could strengthen household-facing interest-rate pass-through, but it could also raise banks’ funding costs, compress net interest margins, and affect loan supply. The proposal will distinguish between a “competition channel,” in which CBDCs improve pass-through by disciplining banks’ deposit pricing, and a “disintermediation channel,” in which CBDCs impair credit supply by reducing stable deposit funding.\n\nA potential identification strategy would exploit cross-country and within-country variation in CBDC rollout intensity, pilot access, wallet limits, and remuneration design. For example, a difference-in-differences design could compare banks more exposed to CBDC adoption—because they operate in regions, customer segments, or payment markets with higher digital-wallet take-up—to less exposed banks before and after CBDC pilot implementation. The main outcomes would include deposit growth, uninsured deposit shares, deposit beta to policy rates, deposit outflow volatility, loan growth, and lending-rate pass-through. Where available, event-study designs around CBDC announcements, pilot expansions, or changes in holding limits could test whether deposit flows and bank equity valuations respond differently for banks with larger retail deposit franchises or weaker liquidity positions.\n\nKey data sources would include central bank CBDC pilot records, wallet adoption statistics, payment transaction data, bank balance-sheet and income-statement data, deposit-rate datasets, supervisory liquidity reports, and monetary policy announcement dates. Cross-country analysis could use BIS, IMF, World Bank Global Findex, and central bank publications, while bank-level analysis could use regulatory filings such as Call Reports in the United States, EBA transparency data in Europe, or comparable supervisory datasets in CBDC pilot jurisdictions. The project would contribute to CBDC design debates by estimating whether safeguards such as holding caps, tiered remuneration, or non-remuneration preserve deposit stability while still allowing CBDCs to improve payment efficiency and monetary policy transmission."}
```

基于检索结果，我提出一个面向自动驾驶场景的多模态感知鲁棒性提升研究方案，强调“感知退化识别—可靠性加权融合—自适应校准—安全验证”的闭环。  
```action
{"tool":"propose","gene_genome":{"mechanism_genome":"本研究拟构建一种面向自动驾驶的鲁棒多模态感知框架，将相机、激光雷达和毫米波雷达统一到 BEV 表征空间中，并引入模态可靠性估计、跨模态一致性约束和不确定性校准机制。核心方法包括：1）设计传感器退化建模与数据增强策略，模拟雨雾雪、夜间眩光、遮挡、点云稀疏、雷达噪声和局部传感器失效；2）提出动态模态可信度网络，根据图像质量、点云密度、时空一致性和预测熵自动评估不同模态在当前场景下的可靠性；3）构建鲁棒 BEV 融合模块，在特征级和决策级同时进行可靠性加权融合，避免低质量模态对整体感知结果产生负迁移；4）引入跨模态一致性学习与测试时自适应，使模型在未知天气、陌生城市和长尾交通场景中仍能保持稳定性能；5）通过不确定性估计和风险感知后处理，为下游规划控制提供可信感知结果。","niche_genome":"该提案面向自动驾驶开放环境中的多模态感知鲁棒性问题，重点覆盖城市道路、高速公路、复杂交叉口、夜间和恶劣天气等真实部署场景。相比仅追求标准数据集精度的感知模型，本研究关注传感器退化、模态缺失、域偏移和长尾目标带来的安全风险，适用于自动驾驶车辆、低速无人配送车、智能路侧感知系统以及车路协同场景。","observation_genome":"现有自动驾驶多模态感知方法在正常天气和标准测试集上已取得较高精度，但在真实道路中常遭遇相机过曝或低照度、激光雷达点云稀疏、毫米波雷达虚警、多传感器时间戳偏移和标定误差等问题。传统融合方法往往默认所有模态始终可靠，导致某一模态退化时错误信息被放大；同时，许多模型缺乏对预测置信度的有效校准，难以让规划模块判断感知结果是否可信。","limitation_genome":"本研究的潜在限制包括：退化模拟与真实极端场景之间仍可能存在分布差异；多模态可靠性估计会增加模型复杂度和推理延迟；跨模态一致性约束依赖较高质量的同步与标定数据；测试时自适应若缺少安全约束，可能在连续域漂移下引入不稳定更新。因此需要在精度、鲁棒性、实时性和安全性之间进行系统权衡，并通过闭环仿真与实车数据进行验证。","delta_genome":"相较于现有以静态融合或单一鲁棒增强为主的方法，本研究的增量贡献在于提出一个面向场景变化的动态鲁棒融合范式：不仅识别每种模态是否可用，还量化其当前可信度；不仅在训练阶段进行退化增强，还在测试阶段进行轻量自适应；不仅输出检测、分割或跟踪结果，还输出可被下游决策利用的不确定性与风险指标。","claim_genome":"研究动机：自动驾驶系统需要在开放、复杂且不可控的道路环境中长期稳定运行，多模态感知是其理解周围环境的关键基础。然而，真实场景中的雨雾雪、夜间光照变化、传感器遮挡、硬件老化、标定偏差和长尾交通参与者会显著削弱感知模型可靠性。现有方法大多在标准数据集上优化平均精度，却较少系统处理模态退化和不确定性传播问题。因此，本研究旨在提升自动驾驶多模态感知系统在复杂环境和异常条件下的鲁棒性、可信性与安全可用性。核心方法：本研究将构建一个基于 BEV 表征的相机-激光雷达-毫米波雷达融合框架。首先，建立面向自动驾驶的多模态退化基准，覆盖图像模糊、低照度、雨雾遮挡、点云稀疏、雷达噪声、传感器缺失和时空不同步等扰动。其次，设计模态可靠性估计模块，从输入质量、跨模态一致性、历史轨迹稳定性和预测不确定性等维度评估每个模态在当前场景下的可信程度。再次，提出可靠性驱动的 BEV 融合网络，在特征融合阶段抑制低质量模态，在决策融合阶段利用不确定性校准输出更可信的目标检测、语义分割和多目标跟踪结果。最后，结合跨模态自监督一致性学习、长尾场景重采样和安全约束下的测试时自适应，提高模型对未知城市、未知天气和传感器异常的泛化能力。预期贡献：1）形成一套面向自动驾驶真实退化条件的多模态鲁棒性感知评测协议与数据增强体系；2）提出动态模态可靠性建模方法，解决传统融合模型在单一模态失效时性能急剧下降的问题；3）构建鲁棒 BEV 融合与不确定性校准框架，为下游规划控制提供可解释、可量化的感知风险；4）在公开数据集和闭环仿真平台上验证该方法在正常场景、恶劣天气、传感器缺失和跨域场景中的性能优势；5）为自动驾驶系统从“高精度感知”走向“可信鲁棒感知”提供方法基础和工程参考。"}}
```

topic: [v15/en/free_text_answer/economics_finance] Write a free-text proposal investigating whether fintech lending expands credit access for underserved borrowers or increases default risk through weaker screen
full_prompt: Write a free-text proposal investigating whether fintech lending expands credit access for underserved borrowers or increases default risk through weaker screening. Specify hypotheses, methodology, and potential limitations.
completion:
I will first search for relevant empirical evidence on fintech lending, credit access, screening, and default risk to anchor the proposal in the existing literature.

```action
{"tool":"search","query":"fintech lending credit access underserved borrowers default risk screening empirical evidence"}
```

[result]: Recent empirical finance studies find mixed evidence. Some papers show that fintech lenders expand mortgage or consumer credit access by using alternative data, automated underwriting, and lower-cost digital distribution, especially for borrowers underserved by traditional banks. Other studies suggest fintech lenders may price risk effectively and process loans faster, but concerns remain that rapid origination, limited relationship banking, or model opacity could weaken screening and increase delinquency or default in some borrower segments.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project investigates whether fintech lending expands credit access for underserved borrowers or instead increases default risk through weaker screening. The central research question is whether fintech lenders improve financial inclusion by lending to borrowers who would otherwise be constrained by traditional banks, and whether any expansion in access is accompanied by higher ex post loan performance problems. The study will focus on consumer or small-business lending markets where both fintech and traditional lenders operate.\n\nThe main hypotheses are as follows. H1: fintech lenders are more likely than traditional lenders to originate loans to underserved borrowers, such as borrowers with thin credit files, lower incomes, minority status, limited collateral, or residence in areas with fewer bank branches. H2: conditional on observable borrower risk, fintech-originated loans do not have higher default rates than comparable bank-originated loans, implying that fintech screening technologies offset the risks of broader access. H3: if fintech expansion is associated with higher default, this effect will be concentrated among borrowers with limited traditional credit histories, suggesting that looser screening or model miscalibration may be present in marginal borrower segments.\n\nThe methodology would combine loan-level data from fintech and traditional lenders with borrower characteristics, credit scores, loan terms, geographic variables, and subsequent repayment outcomes. The empirical strategy would estimate models of loan approval, interest rates, and default using borrower-risk controls and local market fixed effects. To address selection bias, the study could exploit variation in fintech market entry across counties or changes in state-level fintech regulation, using a difference-in-differences design. A complementary matching approach would compare fintech and bank borrowers with similar observable risk profiles to assess whether fintech loans perform differently after origination.\n\nThe expected contribution is to distinguish between two competing interpretations of fintech lending: financial inclusion through improved information technology versus risk expansion through weaker underwriting. If fintech lenders serve underserved borrowers without materially higher default rates, the findings would support policies encouraging responsible fintech entry and data-driven underwriting. If expanded access comes with significantly higher default, the results would highlight the need for stronger consumer protection, model validation, and monitoring of lending standards.\n\nPotential limitations include limited access to proprietary underwriting variables, unobserved borrower characteristics, and the difficulty of determining whether rejected applicants at traditional banks would truly have been creditworthy. Fintech lenders may also differ across platforms, making broad generalization difficult. Finally, macroeconomic conditions can affect default outcomes, so the analysis should test robustness across economic cycles and distinguish normal-period performance from stress-period performance."}
```

Policy discussions emphasize circuit breakers, minimum resting times, market-maker obligations, order-to-trade ratio monitoring, and improved regulatory access to order-level data.

```action
{"tool":"propose","answer":"This project studies how algorithmic trading affects market liquidity during periods of financial stress. The central research question is whether algorithmic traders stabilize markets by supplying liquidity when it is most valuable, or instead withdraw and amplify liquidity shortages when volatility rises. The project will distinguish between normal trading conditions and stress episodes, such as the 2010 Flash Crash, the COVID-19 market turmoil of March 2020, Treasury-market stress events, and other high-volatility windows. The main hypothesis is that algorithmic trading improves liquidity in ordinary times but makes liquidity more fragile during stress because liquidity provision becomes faster, more correlated, and more likely to disappear when adverse-selection risk increases.\n\nThe empirical design will use market microstructure measures at high frequency. Key liquidity outcomes will include quoted spreads, effective spreads, realized spreads, bid and ask depth, order-book imbalance, price impact, Amihud illiquidity, Kyle’s lambda, order cancellation rates, message traffic, and resiliency, measured as the speed with which depth and spreads recover after a shock. Stress will be measured using volatility spikes, large intraday price moves, VIX changes, funding-market indicators, and event-study windows around known episodes. Algorithmic trading intensity can be proxied by message-to-trade ratios, cancellation rates, short order durations, colocation-sensitive activity, or exchange-provided trader classifications where available.\n\nPossible datasets include TAQ for U.S. equities, NASDAQ TotalView-ITCH or LOBSTER limit-order-book data for order-level equity analysis, CME order book data for futures, FINRA TRACE for corporate bonds, and BrokerTec or similar datasets for U.S. Treasuries. If regulatory access is possible, audit-trail data such as CAT or exchange participant identifiers would allow sharper identification of algorithmic and high-frequency traders. The empirical strategy would combine event studies with panel regressions comparing securities or venues with different levels of algorithmic trading exposure before, during, and after stress episodes. Identification could be strengthened using exchange technology changes, tick-size changes, speed bumps, or outages as quasi-exogenous variation in algorithmic trading intensity.\n\nThe expected contribution is to clarify the conditional effect of algorithmic trading on liquidity: beneficial in normal markets but potentially destabilizing when risk constraints bind and algorithms withdraw simultaneously. Policy implications are likely to be nuanced. Rather than restricting algorithmic trading broadly, regulators may focus on stress-period safeguards, including calibrated circuit breakers, enhanced market-maker obligations, order-to-trade ratio surveillance, kill-switch requirements, transparency around order cancellations, and improved real-time monitoring of market depth. The project would help determine whether policy should prioritize speed controls, liquidity-provider commitments, or better data access for regulators during market stress."}
```
